whatIsExif.title: What is EXIF?
whatIsExif.desc: What EXIF means: embedded photo metadata (device, time, GPS)—not a new image format. Exif specification is maintained by JEITA/CIPA.
whatIsExif.content:
Ever scroll through your photos and wonder how your phone knows exactly when a shot was taken, which lens was used, or even where you were? That’s EXIF at work.

If you landed here from searches like **what is EXIF**, **what does EXIF mean**, **what is EXIF information**, or **what is EXIF format**: in plain language, **EXIF information** is the hidden block of tags inside many photo files; **EXIF** (capital “E”) is the standard that names those tags. People often say “format,” but technically EXIF is a **metadata standard** layered on top of image container formats such as JPEG or TIFF—not a replacement for JPEG itself.

EXIF stands for Exchangeable Image File Format. Despite the name, it isn’t a new image format—your file is still a JPEG or TIFF. EXIF is the set of rules for what gets “tagged” onto that file and how, so that different devices and apps can all read the same information.

According to the [Library of Congress – Exif format family](https://www.loc.gov/preservation/digital/formats/fdd/fdd000618.shtml) and other authoritative descriptions of the Exif specification, those tags can include: camera or phone model (Make, Model), shutter speed, aperture, exposure time, date and time, resolution, color space, copyright and description, GPS location, and even an embedded thumbnail.

So EXIF is a metadata standard, not an image file format: the image is still stored as JPEG, TIFF, or (for sound) WAV; EXIF defines how metadata is organized and named inside those files.

The standard was first developed by the Japan Electronic Industry Development Association (JEIDA), with Version 1 published in 1995–1996. In 2000, JEIDA merged with EIAJ to form the Japan Electronics and Information Technology Industries Association (JEITA). Since 2009, the Exif standard has been jointly maintained by JEITA and the Camera & Imaging Products Association (CIPA).

The specification is fully public and widely adopted by digital cameras, smartphones, and printers—which is why a single snapshot from your phone already carries so much hidden detail.

To see how the standard evolved over time, read [“History of EXIF”](/guides/exif-history); to see which file types can carry EXIF, read [“Which formats contain EXIF?”](/guides/supported-formats).

exifHistory.title: History of EXIF
exifHistory.desc: Version evolution from 1.0 (1995) to 3.1: GPS, serials, UTF-8, and more; CIPA/JEITA jointly maintain the standard.
exifHistory.content:
The EXIF (Exchangeable Image File Format) standard dates back to October 1995, when the Japan Electronic Industry Development Association (JEIDA) published Version 1.0, defining a unified way for digital cameras to record image data in files. According to CIPA’s update history and the Library of Congress’s format description, the specification has evolved through many revisions over the following decades. If you’re new to EXIF, you may want to read [“What is EXIF?”](/guides/what-is-exif) first.

Key version milestones:

- 1.0 (1995): First edition; method of recording image data in files.
- 2.0 (1997): GPS Info IFD added for location data—EXIF began to answer “where was this taken?”
- 2.1 (1998): Extended to audio: the method of writing audio data in files; EXIF was no longer still images only.
- 2.2 (2002): ImageUniqueID (to prove image uniqueness), Exif Print, and related tags; from 2.2 onward, sRGB and Adobe RGB color identification are supported.
- 2.3 (2010): Tags for camera owner and serial number to attest image origin.
- 2.31 (2016): UTC time offset and environmental tags (temperature, humidity, pressure).
- 2.32 (2019): Revisions including composite image support.
- 3.0 (2023): Major revision—UTF-8/Unicode, box-structured data, AI annotation, image authenticity; many tag definitions added or changed (e.g. Title, Photographer, Artist, Software).
- 3.1 (2026): Current latest (CIPA DC-008-Translation-2026).

The standard is jointly maintained by CIPA and JEITA. Update history and specification documents are on the CIPA website.

The specification requires that newer readers support earlier versions and skip unknown extensions, ensuring backward compatibility. For which file formats can store EXIF in each version, see [“Which formats contain EXIF?”](/guides/supported-formats).

supportedFormats.title: Which formats contain EXIF?
supportedFormats.desc: How JPEG/TIFF/WAV carry EXIF by spec; HEIC, RAW, WebP compatibility and this site’s support.
supportedFormats.content:
[“What is EXIF?”](/guides/what-is-exif) explains that EXIF is a metadata standard, not a file format. The EXIF (Exchangeable Image File Format) specification explicitly defines two kinds of carriers: image files (TIFF- and JFIF/JPEG-based) and audio (WAV). According to the Library of Congress’s description of the Exif family and CIPA standard documents, the formats that “contain EXIF” in the defined way are primarily JPEG and TIFF.

• **JPEG**: Exif data is stored in JPEG’s predefined APP1 application segment (segment marker 0xFFE1); that segment acts as a container for a TIFF-like structure holding a full set of Exif tags. Most camera and phone JPEGs include Exif—camera model, date/time, aperture and shutter, resolution, optional thumbnail, and more.

• **TIFF**: The TIFF private tag 0x8769 establishes the Exif sub-IFD (Image File Directory) for Exif-specific tags; 0x8825 is used for the GPS sub-IFD (latitude, longitude, altitude, etc.); the spec also defines an Interoperability IFD within the Exif sub-IFD via tag 0xA005.

• **WAV**: From [Exif 2.1](/guides/exif-history) onward, the specification covers Exif audio (WAVE) recording; the declared filename extensions in the Exif spec include jpg, tif, and wav.

Exif-style metadata also often appears in formats that do not strictly follow the spec—for example HEIC/HEIF (used by Apple and others), which can carry Exif-compatible camera parameters, GPS, and timestamps inside an ISOBMFF container; some RAW, AVIF, or WebP files may embed similar data as implemented by vendors or software.

On this site (ExifStrip): we support reading and displaying EXIF for JPEG, PNG, TIFF, HEIC, AVIF, WebP, etc.; preset-based stripping that leaves pixels unchanged is supported mainly for JPEG; other formats typically require re-encoding to remove metadata.

See the homepage “Supported formats” for current support.

exifStructure.title: How is EXIF structured?
exifStructure.desc: EXIF is organized in TIFF-style directories: 0th IFD, Exif sub-IFD, GPS sub-IFD, Interop IFD, and how tags/values are stored.
exifStructure.lead: EXIF inside a photo file isn’t a flat blob—it’s organized as directories, tags, and values. The diagram below summarizes how EXIF sits inside the file so you can get the big picture before reading the details.
exifStructure.content:
If you searched for **JPEG EXIF format** or **where EXIF is in a JPEG**, the short answer is: after the JPEG SOI marker, metadata usually lives in an **APP1** segment (marker `0xFFE1`) that wraps a TIFF-style EXIF tree—not in the compressed image bytes themselves.

EXIF metadata is organized in a TIFF-style directory structure. In JPEG files the entire EXIF block lives in the APP1 application segment (marker `0xFFE1`), effectively embedding a TIFF; in TIFF files the same IFD mechanism is used directly. According to the Library of Congress format description, the main directories are as follows.

## 0th IFD (main image file directory)

Pointed to by the file header or an offset inside APP1; holds tags for the primary image, such as width, height, and orientation.

## Exif sub-IFD

Pointed to by TIFF private tag 0x8769 (Exif IFD) in the 0th IFD; holds Exif-specific tags—capture parameters, device info, date/time, and the like. See [Device and camera info](/guides/dimension-device), [Capture settings](/guides/dimension-settings), and [Date and time](/guides/dimension-datetime).

## GPS sub-IFD

Pointed to by private tag 0x8825 in the 0th IFD; holds latitude, longitude, altitude, GPS timestamps, etc.; included in the spec from [Exif 2.0](/guides/exif-history) onward.

## Interoperability IFD

Inside the Exif sub-IFD, defined by tag 0xA005; used for compatibility-related data.

---

Each directory is a set of tag–value pairs; readers use tag IDs and types to parse. For the definition of EXIF and which formats carry it, see [What is EXIF?](/guides/what-is-exif) and [Which formats contain EXIF?](/guides/supported-formats).

dimensionDevice.title: Device and camera info
dimensionDevice.desc: Make/Model, body and lens serials, Software, firmware; written by device or software—serials are sensitive.
dimensionDevice.content:
Device and camera EXIF records the imaging device and software; it is usually written at capture or save by the camera, phone, or software. The Library of Congress groups this under "camera settings" and related metadata.

## Common tags

Aligned with this site’s device dimension: Make and Model for manufacturer and model; BodySerialNumber and CameraSerialNumber; LensMake and LensModel; Software; FirmwareVersion.

These appear in the 0th IFD and Exif sub-IFD per the CIPA/JEITA standard and common practice.

## Use and caution

Uses include device attribution and statistics; serial numbers can uniquely identify a device and are sensitive—consider stripping before sharing. See [Which EXIF fields are sensitive?](/guides/sensitive-fields) for more.

dimensionSettings.title: Capture settings
dimensionSettings.desc: Shutter, aperture, ISO, focal length, white balance, flash, exposure program; written by the device at capture.
dimensionSettings.content:
Capture settings record exposure and optical conditions at the moment of capture; they are written by the device and live mainly in the Exif sub-IFD. The Library of Congress classifies them as "camera settings" and "technical image data."

## Typical tags

ExposureTime (shutter, e.g. 1/125 s); FNumber (aperture, e.g. f/2.8); ISOSpeedRatings; FocalLength (mm); WhiteBalance; Flash; ExposureProgram (e.g. program, aperture priority); ExposureBiasValue; MeteringMode; SceneCaptureType.

## In plain terms

Aperture controls light and depth of field; shutter and ISO determine exposure; focal length affects angle of view. These fields support analysis or reproduction of shooting conditions and, together with [Image properties](/guides/dimension-image) (resolution, color space), describe how a single image was produced.

**Tag-level detail:** how **FNumber**, **ExposureTime**, **ISOSpeedRatings**, and **ExposureBiasValue** appear in EXIF and relate to photography terms—see [FNumber, ExposureTime, ISO in EXIF](/guides/exif-aperture-shutter-iso-tags). For the **Flash** tag’s numeric/bitmask meaning, see [EXIF Flash tag: bitmask](/guides/exif-flash-tag-bitmask).

dimensionLocation.title: Location (GPS)
dimensionLocation.desc: EXIF GPS: lat/lon, altitude, GPS timestamps in the GPS sub-IFD; GPSAltitudeRef tells above/below sea level—highly sensitive.
dimensionLocation.content:
**EXIF GPS** metadata lives in a separate GPS sub-IFD, pointed to by private tag 0x8825 in the 0th IFD. According to the Library of Congress, GPS support was added in Exif 2.0 to record where a photo was taken.

## Common tags

GPSLatitude and GPSLongitude with GPSLatitudeRef and GPSLongitudeRef (N/S, E/W); in the spec these are degree-minute-second rationals, convertible to decimal degrees for mapping.

GPSAltitude and **GPSAltitudeRef**; GPSDateStamp and GPSTimeStamp; optional speed and direction tags.

## GPSAltitudeRef (and GPSAltitude)

**GPSAltitude** stores height as a rational value. **GPSAltitudeRef** is a one-byte tag that tells readers whether that altitude is **above sea level** (commonly `0`) or **below sea level** (`1`). Software should interpret the pair together. Even without a street address, altitude can hint at terrain or floor level—treat it as **sensitive together with lat/lon** when you review files before sharing. See [Which EXIF fields are sensitive?](/guides/sensitive-fields). Tag names are summarized on [ExifTool’s GPS tag page](https://exiftool.org/TagNames/GPS.html); authoritative definitions are in the CIPA/JEITA Exif specification.

For **reference tags** (**GPSLatitudeRef**, **GPSLongitudeRef**, **GPSAltitudeRef**) and how they pair with numeric latitude, longitude, and altitude, see [EXIF GPS reference tags](/guides/exif-gps-reference-tags).

## Privacy note

Devices with positioning (cameras or phones) write this at capture. Coordinates and timestamps can pinpoint place and time and are highly sensitive; review or strip before sharing. See [Why care about EXIF privacy?](/guides/why-care-privacy).

dimensionDatetime.title: Date and time
dimensionDatetime.desc: Capture, modification, and digitization time; common format has no timezone; some devices use extension fields.
dimensionDatetime.content:
Date and time EXIF records when a photo was taken, when the file was modified, and when it was digitized—used for ordering, search, and provenance. The Library of Congress lists it under "date and time information" in Exif metadata.

## Common tags and format

DateTimeOriginal (capture), DateTime (modification), DateTimeDigitized. Format is typically "YYYY:MM:DD HH:MM:SS" (e.g. 2024:03:15 14:30:00) with no timezone field.

## Timezone limitation

The standard date/time tags do not include UTC offset. Some devices or software use extensions such as OffsetTime or OffsetTimeOriginal; not all files have them. Exif 2.31 added support for UTC offset; see [History of EXIF](/guides/exif-history) for version details.

For a **side-by-side** explanation of **DateTimeOriginal**, **DateTime**, and **DateTimeDigitized** (common confusion in viewers and DAM tools), see [DateTimeOriginal vs DateTime in EXIF](/guides/exif-datetimeoriginal-vs-datetime).

dimensionImage.title: Image properties
dimensionImage.desc: Width, height, resolution, color space, bit depth; used for display/print; they do not change pixel data.
dimensionImage.content:
Image-property EXIF describes geometry, resolution, color, and encoding—how the image is displayed or printed. The Library of Congress notes that image-related metadata includes pixel count, aspect ratio (required 1:1 in the spec), and sampling (e.g. RGB 4:4:4, Y:Cb:Cr 4:2:2 or 4:2:0).

## Common tags

ImageWidth and ImageLength; XResolution, YResolution, ResolutionUnit (e.g. DPI); ColorSpace (e.g. sRGB); BitsPerSample, SamplesPerPixel; Compression, PhotometricInterpretation; ExifVersion, FlashPixVersion.

## Purpose

These allow correct sizing and color handling without changing pixel data. The container format (see [Which formats contain EXIF?](/guides/supported-formats)) defines how image data is encoded; EXIF only describes the embedded metadata.

dimensionOther.title: Artist, copyright, and comments
dimensionOther.desc: Artist, Copyright, UserComment, ImageDescription, MakerNote; the first are often read/write; MakerNote is vendor-private.
dimensionOther.content:
Artist, copyright, and comment EXIF are descriptive metadata; the Library of Congress groups them as "descriptive metadata" and "copyright." Some are user- or software-editable; others are written by the device or vendor.

## Common tags

Artist, Copyright, UserComment, ImageDescription, MakerNote.

Artist, Copyright, UserComment, and ImageDescription are typically read/writable in most software; MakerNote is vendor-defined, often undocumented, and only fully interpreted by vendor tools—removing or keeping it does not affect standard compliance.

## Version and privacy

Exif 3.0 added or changed definitions for Title, Photographer, ImageEditor, Artist, Software, and others. UserComment and ImageDescription may contain personal data; check before sharing. See [Which EXIF fields are sensitive?](/guides/sensitive-fields).

iptcXmpOverview.title: IPTC, XMP, and EXIF: how they fit together
iptcXmpOverview.desc: IPTC vs EXIF vs XMP: three metadata families in one file; stripping EXIF may not remove IPTC captions—preview before sharing.
iptcXmpOverview.content:
People often say "EXIF" when they mean "everything hidden in the photo file." In practice, cameras and apps may store **several families** of metadata: **EXIF** (capture settings, GPS, dates), **IPTC** (news-style captions, copyright notices, keywords), and **XMP** (flexible, XML-based tags that Adobe and others use for titles, rights, and more). Tools like [ExifTool](https://exiftool.org/) document each family under its own tag namespace—handy when you want to go deep.

## How they differ (in one paragraph)

**EXIF** is the classic camera/phone block: aperture, ISO, lens, GPS, DateTimeOriginal, etc. **IPTC** historically served news agencies; you still see Caption, Credit, Keywords in many workflows. **XMP** can duplicate some EXIF/IPTC fields but also carries Dublin Core–style fields and app-specific data. The same *idea* (e.g. copyright) might appear in more than one block—that is normal, not a bug.

## IPTC vs EXIF (at a glance)

| | **EXIF** | **IPTC** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Typical writer** | Camera / phone at capture | Editors, newsrooms, DAM tools |
| **Typical content** | Aperture, ISO, focal length, GPS, DateTimeOriginal | Caption, Headline, Keywords, Credit |
| **Same JPEG?** | Often **both** blocks are present | Yes—removing only EXIF may **leave IPTC** (and XMP) behind |

So **IPTC vs EXIF** is not “pick one”: they solve different jobs and can coexist. If you still see captions or keywords after an “EXIF strip,” check IPTC/XMP in your viewer.

## XMP vs EXIF

**XMP** (Extensible Metadata Platform) embeds metadata as **XML** in the file (common in JPEG, PNG, TIFF). **EXIF** embeds camera-centric tags in a **TIFF IFD** structure (typically inside a JPEG **APP1** segment). They are **different containers**—searches like **xmp vs exif** or **remove XMP vs remove EXIF** point to the same issue: **clearing EXIF does not always clear XMP** (or IPTC).

| | **EXIF** | **XMP** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Typical content** | Aperture, ISO, GPS, DateTimeOriginal, device | Rights, title, keywords, Lightroom/Camera Raw hooks, duplicate copyright |
| **After a narrow “EXIF-only” strip** | Often removed or reduced | **May remain** unless the tool rewrites XMP segments |

For a practical workflow: [view](/guides/how-to-view) the full metadata panel, then [edit or strip](/guides/how-to-edit-strip) with a tool that matches what you need removed. Power users may use ExifTool with the appropriate tag groups; always preview the output file.

## What this means for stripping and privacy

Removing "EXIF" in a narrow sense might **not** remove every caption or keyword stored in IPTC or XMP. Always **preview** what your tool actually shows before you share. On [ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com) we surface parsed metadata by dimension; for JPEG, preset stripping targets the metadata segments we support—see [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip) and [Supported image formats on ExifStrip](/blog/product-supported-formats).

## Read more

- [What is EXIF?](/guides/what-is-exif)
- [How EXIF is structured](/guides/exif-structure)
- [Is EXIF the same as "metadata"?](/blog/topic-exif-vs-metadata)

makerNotesBasics.title: What are camera maker notes (MakerNote)?
makerNotesBasics.desc: Vendor-specific EXIF blobs (Canon, Nikon, Sony, …) explain extra fields; they are not "wrong EXIF"—just private extensions.
makerNotesBasics.content:
Besides the standard EXIF tags you see in specs, many files contain a **MakerNote** (manufacturer note): a block of data that **only the camera vendor** fully understands. That is why the same scene from a Canon and a Sony can show different "extra" rows in a metadata viewer.

## Why MakerNote exists

Vendors pack autofocus details, internal serials, lens corrections, and dozens of proprietary fields into MakerNote. Generic standards do not define those tags, so they live in a private area. Reference tools such as [ExifTool](https://exiftool.org/TagNames/index.html) publish many **per-brand** MakerNote interpretations.

## Privacy

MakerNote is usually **less obvious** than GPS in social risk, but it can still carry **serials, firmware hints, or internal IDs**. If you strip "device" or "privacy" presets on a site that rewrites JPEG metadata, MakerNote may be removed along with standard device tags—behavior depends on implementation. When in doubt, **view first**, then choose a preset. See [Sensitive fields](/guides/sensitive-fields).

## On this site

We group some vendor-specific data with **Device** or related dimensions when parsers expose it. For background on standard vs vendor tags, see [What's in EXIF — device](/guides/dimension-device) and [Artist, copyright, and comments](/guides/dimension-other) (MakerNote is mentioned there too).

orientationTagExplained.title: EXIF Orientation: what it does (and what it doesn't)
orientationTagExplained.desc: Orientation values 1–8 tell viewers how to rotate the image for display; they do not always mean pixels were physically rotated.
orientationTagExplained.content:
If you searched for **orientation EXIF**, **EXIF Orientation values 1–8**, or **what is orientation in EXIF**, this page is the right place: the **Orientation** tag (often shown under image settings) tells software **how to display** the JPEG—rotation and mirroring—without necessarily changing stored pixels. Values **1–8** map to different combinations (0°, 90°, 180°, 270°, and flips). A well-behaved viewer rotates the picture **for display**; the compressed pixel grid may still be stored in the sensor's native orientation. The tag name and IFD placement match our [EXIF tag quick reference](/guides/tag-reference) and [LOC TIFF/Exif tag lists](https://www.loc.gov/preservation/digital/formats/content/tiff_tags.shtml).

## EXIF Orientation values 1–8 (reference)

| Value | Typical meaning (display) |
| --- | --- |
| 1 | Normal (0°) |
| 2 | Flipped horizontally |
| 3 | Rotated 180° |
| 4 | Flipped vertically |
| 5 | Flipped horizontally + rotated 270° CW (or 90° CCW) |
| 6 | Rotated 90° CW |
| 7 | Flipped horizontally + rotated 90° CW |
| 8 | Rotated 90° CCW |

Exact wording in the spec uses row/column; viewers implement the same eight cases. Searching **orientation exif** or **exif orientation 1–8** refers to this tag.

## Common confusion

- **"My file looks wrong in one app"** — Some apps ignore Orientation and show raw pixels; others honor the tag. Same file, different look.
- **"If I edit Orientation, did I rotate the photo?"** — Changing only the tag adjusts the **interpretation**; truly re-encoding the image to bake rotation into pixels is a separate step (export/re-save in an editor).

## EXIF Orientation vs quality

Updating Orientation in metadata **does not** recompress the image; it is a small metadata change. Stripping Orientation may cause some viewers to show the file "sideways" again—decide based on how you will share the file.

## Learn more

- [Image settings dimension](/guides/dimension-settings)
- [How to view EXIF](/guides/how-to-view)

Formal tag lists appear in CIPA/JEITA docs and references such as ExifTool's [EXIF tag page](https://exiftool.org/TagNames/EXIF.html).

whyCarePrivacy.title: Why care about EXIF privacy?
whyCarePrivacy.desc: Photos often carry GPS and device info by default; it’s invisible in the gallery but readable with tools—check and strip before sharing.
whyCarePrivacy.content:
A lot of people assume they're only sending a picture—but the file usually carries a bunch of invisible baggage: EXIF. Phones and cameras write GPS, model name, and capture time into the file by default. When you copy the file to a PC, upload to the cloud, or send it in a chat, that metadata typically goes along for the ride.

**Why does it slip under the radar?** Because your gallery doesn't show it. EXIF is just part of the file: any system info panel, image viewer, or online EXIF tool can read latitude, longitude, timestamps, and sometimes even serial numbers. Below are two example screenshots of parsed EXIF:

![EXIF parsing example 1](/images/guides/why-care-privacy/0ad3b346-6904-4671-8c10-50eabd569275.png)

![EXIF parsing example 2](/images/guides/why-care-privacy/e3db8d12-1776-4167-9fc9-5a3cf04738a6.png)

Do social networks and file hosts strip it on upload? Some do, some don't, and they rarely spell it out—you can't rely on "the platform will handle it."

**What's the fallout?** Anyone with the original file can infer where and when you shot it and what device you used. That's enough for profiling, stalking, or doxxing; in commercial contexts it can feed user targeting. Common counter-example: you post a "check-in" photo as the original file in a group; the EXIF includes precise GPS, so everyone who can save the image can see your regular spots or even your address.

So: glance at what's in the file before you share, and strip what you don't want to expose. For which fields are risky, see [Which EXIF fields are sensitive?](/guides/sensitive-fields); for how to choose "location only" vs "remove all," see [What to do before sharing?](/guides/before-you-share).

sensitiveFields.title: Which EXIF fields are sensitive?
sensitiveFields.desc: By risk level: location and serials, time and model, free text; why they matter and when they can backfire.
sensitiveFields.content:
Whether something is "sensitive" depends on who gets the file and what they do with it—the same EXIF can be low risk in one context and high in another. Here's a rough order from highest to lower risk.

**High: location and unique device IDs**

[Location (GPS)](/guides/dimension-location) fields—latitude, longitude, altitude, GPS timestamps—pinpoint where the photo was taken, often to within meters. If the original gets out, others can see where you were; with several photos they can infer routines, home, or workplace. Camera or lens serial numbers (e.g. CameraSerialNumber, BodySerialNumber) in EXIF can uniquely identify your gear and tie to warranty, accounts, or second-hand history; they've been used in scams or harassment. Below is an example of high-sensitivity fields parsed from a photo:

![High-sensitivity fields example](/images/guides/sensitive-fields/8023c07e-def7-4ae7-8183-96a31ef94dbd.png)

**Medium: time and camera model**

[Date and time](/guides/dimension-datetime) fields (e.g. DateTimeOriginal) reveal when you shot; combined they build a timeline of your activity. Make/Model aren't unique but can suggest device tier and habits and become an identity clue in some situations.

**Lower but don't ignore: free-text**

UserComment and ImageDescription are whatever you or the app typed in—names, places, plans, notes. Sharing without checking is giving that away. Below is an example of medium- and low-sensitivity fields (time, model, free-text):

![Medium/low-sensitivity fields example](/images/guides/sensitive-fields/ef685d0f-95c7-46dc-a592-7a515a38947a.png)

**Counter-example:** A family photo with GPS in the hands of a stranger shows parks and school routes; or serial number plus social profile used to impersonate the owner and recover a device.

Once you know which fields matter, pair that with [What to do before sharing?](/guides/before-you-share) to choose a stripping strategy.

beforeYouShare.title: What to do before sharing?
beforeYouShare.desc: Get in the habit of "view first, then decide": use this site or system properties to check, then choose location-only, device-only, or remove all—matching our presets.
beforeYouShare.content:
There's no universal rule like "never keep EXIF"—it depends who you're sending to and whether the file might be re-shared. A simple habit helps: **look first, then decide**.

**Step 1: Inspect the file**

Use your OS file properties, a desktop EXIF tool, or a [browser-based viewer](/guides/how-to-view) like this site. Check whether the kinds of data in [Which EXIF fields are sensitive?](/guides/sensitive-fields) are present—GPS, serials, capture time, UserComment—and how much. On this site, after upload you can use the privacy analysis block to see which sensitive items exist. Example:

![Privacy analysis example](/images/guides/before-you-share/4d550a07-ed4d-485e-9f6d-6012509120dd.png)

**Step 2: Pick a strategy**

- **Strip location only:** Keep device and time for your own sorting and archives; good when sharing with people you know but you don't want to reveal where you were.
- **Strip device/serials only:** Keep time and location; use when you only care about hiding your gear.
- **Remove all:** Safest when the image goes to strangers or could be reposted; no guesswork about what's left.

The site's [strip presets](/guides/how-to-edit-strip) (e.g. privacy-only, GPS-only, device-only, remove all) map to these; choose what fits. Once you've chosen, select the matching preset and run the strip. Example:

![Strip and remove example](/images/guides/before-you-share/40e336e7-9ff5-4489-94e6-1bc9f88f8ce8.png)

**Counter-example:** Stripping everything without looking, then later wanting capture time for sorting and finding it's gone; or leaving everything in and sending the original to a stranger or a public forum.

In short: look before you send, then choose what to remove. For what this site can do, see [What can ExifStrip do?](/guides/what-exifstrip-does).

howToView.title: How to view EXIF?
howToView.desc: Free online EXIF viewer: view EXIF in your browser with no upload. OS, desktop apps, and ExifStrip—read photo metadata locally by dimension.
howToView.content:
**Free online EXIF viewer:** If you searched for **view EXIF online**, **online EXIF viewer**, **read EXIF without uploading**, or **EXIF viewer in browser**, this page compares built-in OS tools, desktop apps, and **[ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com)**—where EXIF is parsed **locally** in your browser (files are not sent to our servers).

EXIF information is often said to matter a lot—so what is it? EXIF (Exchangeable Image File Format) is a metadata standard embedded in photo files; it records camera model, shutter, aperture, date/time, GPS, and more. The standard is developed and maintained by JEITA (Japan Electronics and Information Technology Industries Association) and CIPA (Camera & Imaging Products Association).

For an official format overview, see the [Library of Congress – Exif family](https://www.loc.gov/preservation/digital/formats/fdd/fdd000618.shtml) and the [CIPA Exif standard](https://www.cipa.jp/e/std/std-sec.html).

This information usually does not appear in normal galleries or thumbnails; you can’t see it just by looking at the image, because it sits behind the photo. In built-in or more advanced software, however, you can view it, and it is useful for checking where a photo came from, reviewing privacy before sharing, or organizing files.

Below are several ways to view the EXIF hidden in your photos.

# **Built into your operating system**

You can view this information without professional software. The steps depend on your OS.

**Windows:** In File Explorer, right-click the image → **Properties** → open the **Details** tab. You’ll see some fields (e.g. camera, dimensions, date taken). What appears varies by Windows version, and not all EXIF fields are shown.

![View EXIF on Windows](/images/guides/how-to-view/window-view.png)

**macOS:** Open the image in **Preview** → menu **Tools** → **Show Inspector** (or Option+Command+I). Use the **More Info** or similar tab to view EXIF; some fields may be missing depending on format or device.

**Phone (Android / iOS):** In the Photos/Gallery app, open the image and look for **Details**, **Info**, or swipe up. You’ll usually see capture date and sometimes location; full EXIF is not always shown. For more, use a file manager or a third‑party EXIF app.

![View EXIF on iOS](/images/guides/how-to-view/ios-view.png)

# **Desktop EXIF software**

For full EXIF, batch viewing, or editing, desktop tools are an option.

**ExifTool:** Command-line tool; reads and writes EXIF for many formats and is used by many apps under the hood. The downside is that it requires the command line, which can be a barrier for some users.

**XnView:** Free, supports many image formats, can view and export EXIF in batch, with a straightforward interface; some advanced features are in the paid version.

**IrfanView** (Windows): Lightweight and fast; can view and edit EXIF. It only runs on Windows.

These tools work offline and are powerful; the downside is they require installation, and support for HEIC, RAW, etc. varies—check compatibility for your file types.

# **Online EXIF viewers**

Many websites let you upload an image and view its EXIF in the browser, with no install. That’s convenient for a one-off check. When you use such a service, the image is sent to their servers; if the photo is private or sensitive, check the site’s privacy policy and how they handle data before uploading.

# **How to view EXIF on this site ([ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com))**

**Step 1:** Open the [ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com) website in your browser and go to the homepage.

![Step 1](/images/guides/how-to-view/7d6ba228-d45f-4dca-8188-e64943375df4.png)

**Step 2:** Click the upload area or drag and drop the image(s) you want to inspect; single or multiple files are supported.

![Step 2](/images/guides/how-to-view/23f33ad8-9927-4bf3-bed7-8479df406acc.png)

**Step 3:** After you select files, the page parses EXIF locally—your images are not uploaded to our servers. When parsing finishes, the **File information** section shows name, dimensions, type, etc.; below that, metadata is grouped by category (device, capture settings, location, date/time, image properties, author/copyright), so you can quickly find what you need.

![Step 3](/images/guides/how-to-view/df78d179-7f68-4aa9-9362-df72046dd366.png)

**Step 4:** If the image has GPS data, the page shows the capture location on a map.

![Step 4](/images/guides/how-to-view/ec7a0466-bd8a-405e-8598-d13f0ea3c053.png)

Advantages of using [ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com): parsing and display happen entirely in your browser, so your images never leave your device, which helps with privacy; no installation; and it can read EXIF from JPEG, PNG, HEIC, WebP, and other common formats.

---

You can choose how to view EXIF based on your habits, whether you need batch handling, and whether you’re comfortable sending files to a third party. If after viewing you want to edit or remove EXIF, see [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip); for a full list of what this site does, see [What can ExifStrip do?](/guides/what-exifstrip-does).

howToEditStrip.title: How to edit or remove EXIF?
howToEditStrip.desc: Edit artist/copyright/time and write back to JPEG; strip by dimension or remove all—JPEG lossless, PNG/WebP re-encode; four presets explained.
howToEditStrip.content:
In JPEG, EXIF metadata typically lives in an [APP1 segment](https://www.loc.gov/preservation/digital/formats/fdd/fdd000147.shtml) alongside the compressed image data—so it can often be changed without re-encoding pixels.

Since EXIF in your photos matters so much, how can you edit or remove it to protect your privacy? This site offers two approaches:

1. **Edit** — Change some EXIF fields (e.g. author, copyright, capture time) and write them back to the file; you can enter false or neutral values to hide your real metadata.

2. **Strip** — Remove some or all metadata; anyone who gets the modified image cannot see or recover the original EXIF.

Below we explain both and how they work on this site.

# **Editing EXIF**

Many tools let you edit certain EXIF fields (e.g. Artist, Copyright, capture time) and save the result back to the file. On this site, JPEGs support editing for some fields; follow the steps below.

**Step 1: Upload and view** — After you [view EXIF](/guides/how-to-view) and upload an image, the page shows metadata grouped by dimension (device, capture settings, author/copyright, date/time, etc.). Locate the dimension block you want to change and continue to the next step.

![Editing step 1: metadata by dimension after upload](/images/guides/how-to-edit-strip/0f0a5151-c932-4fd3-9a5b-594dc67a8977.png)

**Step 2: Click edit and change the value** — In the right dimension (e.g. author/copyright, date/time), find a row that has an edit icon. Click the edit icon on that row, then enter the new value in the dialog (e.g. replace author, copyright, or capture time) to hide real metadata or enter false information.

![Editing step 2: click edit and modify](/images/guides/how-to-edit-strip/0b18359e-a2df-4ca8-9b15-add3b3aac849.png)

**Step 3: Save to write back** — When you’re done, click save; the changes are written back to the image’s EXIF. Not every tag is editable; only fields that the spec allows to be written and that the site supports will show an edit control.

![Editing step 3: save and write back to file](/images/guides/how-to-edit-strip/cb971da1-98c8-427f-8b3e-120842d80445.png)

# **Removing / stripping**

There are two common approaches: strip by **dimension** (e.g. remove only GPS, or only device info, keep the rest), or remove **all** EXIF. On this site you can choose a strip preset and run the strip; an example is shown below.

![Strip / remove EXIF example](/images/guides/how-to-edit-strip/b19298f7-47e7-4612-9772-54ac12e18712.png)

In **JPEG**, EXIF lives in the APP1 segment, separate from the pixel data. Stripping only rewrites or drops that segment and does not touch pixels, so you can get **lossless stripping** with no change in image quality. For **PNG, WebP**, and similar formats there is usually no “header-only” path; removing EXIF typically means decode then re-encode, which can have a very small quality impact. For more, see [Does removing EXIF affect image quality?](/guides/faq-remove-safe). Which formats carry EXIF is described in [Which formats contain EXIF?](/guides/supported-formats).

## remove, strip, delete, scrub: same goal, different words

If you searched for **remove EXIF**, **remove EXIF data**, **delete EXIF**, **EXIF delete**, **stripping EXIF data**, **EXIF scrubber**, or **EXIF deleter**, you usually want the same outcome: the next person who opens the file should **not** see (or recover) the old metadata. “**Scrub**” and “**strip**” are informal verbs vendors use for clearing metadata; **this guide** uses **strip** for what ExifStrip does on JPEG **without re-encoding pixels**. Tools differ in whether they also remove **IPTC/XMP** or require **re-encode** for PNG/WebP—always **preview** what remains after export.

## "Strip data" (ambiguous search term)

The phrase **strip data** alone can mean many things outside photos (logs, tables, APIs). For **image metadata**, align your content with **strip photo metadata**, **strip EXIF**, or **remove metadata from images**—the same workflows as the presets below.

# **Presets on this site**

[ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com) offers four strip presets, aligned with the “strip by dimension” idea:

- **Remove all**: Remove all EXIF from the file.
- **Privacy only**: Remove only location, device, author/copyright, etc.; keep capture settings, date/time, image properties.
- **GPS only**: Remove only location data; keep the rest.
- **Device only**: Remove only device and lens-related data; keep the rest.

An example of the four presets is shown below.

![Strip presets on this site](/images/guides/how-to-edit-strip/ca9fa2cc-90ad-42bd-aa7f-ff6caddb214b.png)

For **JPEG**, using these presets only rewrites metadata and does not change pixels, so quality is unchanged. For **PNG, WebP**, and similar formats, choosing “Remove all” strips metadata by re-encoding; quality impact is usually negligible. **HEIC, TIFF, AVIF** and the like are not supported for preset-based stripping on this site; to remove EXIF from those, you can convert to JPEG first and then strip, or use another tool that supports the format.

---

After choosing a preset, click “Strip” and download to get the processed file. To inspect EXIF before deciding what to remove, see [How to view EXIF?](/guides/how-to-view); for a full list of what this site does, see [What can ExifStrip do?](/guides/what-exifstrip-does).

whatExifstripDoes.title: What can ExifStrip do?
whatExifstripDoes.desc: View (by dimension and map), edit some fields, four strip presets, export CSV/JSON/TXT/HEX, privacy analysis; all local, free, no upload.
whatExifstripDoes.content:
[ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com) is a tool for viewing, editing, and removing EXIF from images in your browser. No installation is required—open the site and you can start; all parsing, editing, and stripping happen on your device, and your images are not uploaded to any server, so it suits anyone who wants to inspect or clean EXIF while keeping privacy in mind. Below is what the site can do, by feature.

# **View**

After you upload or drag and drop an image, the page shows EXIF grouped by dimension: **device** (maker, model, lens, etc.), **capture settings** (aperture, shutter, ISO, focal length, etc.), **location** (GPS), **date/time**, **image properties** (width, height, resolution, color space, etc.), and **author/copyright** (Artist, Copyright, UserComment, etc.). This grouping makes it easy to find a particular kind of data without scanning a flat tag list. The **File information** area at the top shows file name, dimensions, type, and modification time; if the image has GPS data, the capture location is shown on a map so you can cross-check coordinates with the EXIF fields. The site can read and display EXIF from JPEG, PNG, HEIC, WebP, and other common formats. The following shows the dimension-grouped EXIF and file information area.

![EXIF and file information by dimension](/images/guides/what-exifstrip-does/e7a1b2c3-d4e5-4f6a-b7c8-d9e0f1a2b3c4.png)

For step-by-step use, see [How to view EXIF?](/guides/how-to-view).

# **Edit and strip**

**Editing**: Some fields (e.g. Artist, Copyright, capture time) can be changed in the UI; when you save, the changes are written back to the JPEG. That is useful for overwriting real metadata with false or neutral values while keeping the EXIF structure. **Stripping**: Four presets are available—**Remove all** (clear all EXIF), **Privacy only** (remove location, device, author/copyright, etc.), **GPS only**, and **Device only**. For JPEG, preset-based stripping only rewrites the metadata segment and does not touch pixels, so image quality is unchanged; for non-JPEG (e.g. PNG, WebP), choosing Remove all strips metadata by re-encoding, and the impact on quality is usually negligible. HEIC, TIFF, AVIF, and similar formats do not support preset-based stripping at the moment. For details and format behavior, see [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip).

# **Export**

You can export the current image’s EXIF as **CSV**, **JSON**, **TXT**, or **HEX** (raw hexadecimal), as shown below.

![Export EXIF format options](/images/guides/what-exifstrip-does/b3c4d5e6-f7a8-4b9c-0d1e-2f3a4b5c6d7e.png)

CSV is handy for viewing or merging data from multiple images in a spreadsheet; JSON is suitable for programmatic use; TXT is plain text for archiving or search; HEX is a low-level dump for debugging or comparing with the spec. The exported content matches the dimension-grouped data shown on the page.

# **Privacy analysis**

The page automatically detects sensitive items such as GPS, device/lens serial numbers, and capture time, and shows risk levels (e.g. high, medium, low) in the UI.

![File info, GPS and privacy risk indicators](/images/guides/what-exifstrip-does/a2b3c4d5-e6f7-4a8b-9c0d-1e2f3a4b5c6d.png)

That way you can see at a glance what sensitive EXIF the image contains before sharing, and then decide whether to strip only location or remove everything—aligned with [Which EXIF fields are sensitive?](/guides/sensitive-fields) and [What to do before sharing?](/guides/before-you-share), so you can get into the habit of checking before you strip.

---

[ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com) is free and ad-free; you can select multiple images and view or process them one by one. Everything runs locally with a privacy-first design. To learn how to view or strip EXIF step by step, see [How to view EXIF?](/guides/how-to-view) and [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip).

faqRemoveSafe.title: Does removing EXIF affect image quality?
faqRemoveSafe.desc: Metadata-only removal (e.g. our JPEG presets) leaves quality unchanged; re-encoding PNG/WebP may have tiny theoretical differences.
faqRemoveSafe.content:
**Short answer:** If you only remove metadata and don't re-encode (e.g. the site's preset-based strip for JPEG), pixels are untouched and image quality is unchanged. Quality can be affected when EXIF is removed by re-encoding—e.g. decode-then-encode for PNG or WebP—which adds another compression step; in practice the difference is usually negligible.

**Why:** EXIF lives in the file header (e.g. APP1 in JPEG), separate from the compressed image data. Stripping only rewrites or drops those segments and doesn't touch the image stream, so metadata-only removal doesn't affect quality.

**Counter-example:** Assuming "removing EXIF always means re-encoding" and therefore not stripping; or using a tool that does re-encode and expecting pixel-identical output—that depends on format and settings.

For how stripping works here, see [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip); for which formats carry EXIF, see [Which formats contain EXIF?](/guides/supported-formats).

faqWhoWrites.title: Who writes EXIF?
faqWhoWrites.desc: Mainly the capture device at shutter press; editing, export, and platforms may keep, change, or add—final EXIF may not be "camera-original."
faqWhoWrites.content:
**Who writes it?** First, the capture device. When you press the shutter, the camera or phone firmware writes a full set of EXIF: model, [date and time](/guides/dimension-datetime), often [location (GPS)](/guides/dimension-location). So the first answer is: **the device at capture time**.

**Can it be changed later?** Yes. Software (editors, exporters, managers) may keep, alter, or add fields—e.g. Software, timestamp, copyright. After upload, many platforms re-encode or strip EXIF too. So the EXIF you see may be device + several apps; it's not always "what the camera wrote."

**Counter-example:** Treating EXIF as immutable camera evidence without considering edits/exports/uploads; or assuming "no one touched it" when you're not sure of the chain.

For device-related fields, see [Device and camera info](/guides/dimension-device).

faqSocialNetwork.title: Is EXIF still there after posting to social media?
faqSocialNetwork.desc: Does X (Twitter) strip EXIF? Platforms differ; don’t assume posted = gone—strip locally first. See blog for X/Instagram/Meta notes.
faqSocialNetwork.content:
**Short answer:** Many platforms do something to uploads (resize, re-encode, strip some or all EXIF), but **behavior isn't uniform and often isn't documented**. So you can't assume "once I post it, EXIF is gone"; if you want to be sure nothing sensitive goes with the image, clean it yourself before uploading.

**About X (Twitter):** A common search is **does X strip EXIF from uploaded images**. X’s Help Center explains that optional **post location** is separate from location embedded in the image; uploaded photos are **processed**, and **GPS-type EXIF is generally not shown to other users** the way raw embedded tags might suggest—but policies change, and “not shown” is not the same as “never read on the server.” For a longer, sourced comparison (X, Meta, WeChat, etc.), read [How do social platforms handle EXIF in your photos?](/blog/topic-platform-strip).

**Why:** For privacy or policy, some services strip all EXIF, some only for public posts, some only resize and leave metadata. You often can't know the exact rule, and it can change.

**Counter-example:** Posting originals assuming "the platform will strip it" and then having a copy with GPS/timestamps saved by a bot or another user; or the opposite—being too worried to share anything. Sensible middle ground: check EXIF before posting and, if needed, strip by [What to do before sharing?](/guides/before-you-share), then upload.

For why EXIF privacy matters, see [Why care about EXIF privacy?](/guides/why-care-privacy).

faqCanRecover.title: Can I recover EXIF after removal?
faqCanRecover.desc: You cannot recover stripped EXIF from that file; only the original or an unmodified copy still has the metadata.
faqCanRecover.content:
**Short answer:** You **cannot** recover removed EXIF from the **stripped file itself**. There's no recycle bin—once it's gone from that file, it's gone. You can only get it back if you still have an **original backup** (or another copy that wasn't overwritten).

**Why:** Stripping either rewrites/removes the metadata segments or produces a new file by re-encoding. The new file never contained the old EXIF, so nothing can read it back from that file.

**Counter-example:** Realizing after stripping that you needed the capture time or GPS for archives; or overwriting the original and only keeping the stripped version—then it's really gone. So: back up originals before stripping; if you need to keep EXIF long-term, don't overwrite the original with a fully stripped file.

For how stripping works, see [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip).

faqRawHeic.title: What's special about RAW / HEIC EXIF?
faqRawHeic.desc: RAW varies by vendor; HEIC uses ISOBMFF; we can read HEIC EXIF; preset-based precise stripping is mainly for JPEG.
faqRawHeic.content:
**How they differ from JPEG** RAW is vendor-specific; EXIF is often tied to the RAW header and MakerNote, so read/write follows each vendor's spec. HEIC/HEIF use an ISOBMFF container; they can embed EXIF-like metadata (camera, GPS, time), but **embedding and stripping work differently from JPEG**—often involving container edits or re-encode, not "just tweak APP1, pixels unchanged."

**What this site does** The site can **read** EXIF from HEIC and similar formats and show it. **Preset-based stripping** (metadata-only, pixels unchanged) is implemented mainly for JPEG. For RAW/HEIC, removing EXIF usually means re-encoding or other tools, not the same "lossless strip" as JPEG.

**Counter-example:** Expecting the same "no quality loss" as JPEG when stripping RAW/HEIC; or expecting the site to do identical preset stripping for HEIC as for JPEG—currently it doesn't.

For which formats carry EXIF, see [Which formats contain EXIF?](/guides/supported-formats); for site capabilities, see [What can ExifStrip do?](/guides/what-exifstrip-does).

tagReference.title: EXIF tag quick reference
tagReference.desc: EXIF tags this site can parse, grouped by device, capture, location, date/time, artist/copyright, image—with short descriptions.
tagReference.content:
Below is the full tag list, organized by dimension and aligned with [How EXIF is structured](/guides/exif-structure). Each tag maps to `exifBlocks` and the EXIF dimension docs; formal definitions follow CIPA/JEITA and the [LOC Tags for TIFF](https://www.loc.gov/preservation/digital/formats/content/tiff_tags.shtml).

## 1. device (camera/equipment)

| Tag | Description |
|-----|-------------|
| Make | Manufacturer |
| Model | Model |
| BodySerialNumber | Body serial number |
| CameraSerialNumber | Camera serial number |
| LensMake | Lens manufacturer |
| LensModel | Lens model |
| Software | Software |
| FirmwareVersion | Firmware version |

## 2. imageSettings (capture settings)

| Tag | Description |
|-----|-------------|
| Orientation | Orientation |
| ExposureTime | Shutter speed |
| FNumber | Aperture (f-number) |
| FocalLength | Focal length |
| ISOSpeedRatings | ISO speed |
| WhiteBalance | White balance |
| Flash | Flash |
| ExposureProgram | Exposure program |
| ExposureMode | Exposure mode |
| MeteringMode | Metering mode |
| SceneCaptureType | Scene capture type |
| SceneType | Scene type (byte) |
| ExposureBiasValue | Exposure bias |

## 3. location (GPS)

| Tag | Description |
|-----|-------------|
| GPSLatitude | Latitude |
| GPSLongitude | Longitude |
| GPSAltitude | Altitude |
| GPSAltitudeRef | Altitude reference (above/below sea level) |
| GPSDateStamp | GPS date |
| GPSTimeStamp | GPS time |
| GPSLatitudeRef | N/S reference |
| GPSLongitudeRef | E/W reference |
| GPSSpeedRef | Speed unit reference |
| GPSImgDirection | Image direction |
| GPSImgDirectionRef | Direction reference |
| GPSDestBearing | Destination bearing |
| GPSDestBearingRef | Bearing reference |

## 4. dateTime (date and time)

| Tag | Description |
|-----|-------------|
| DateTimeOriginal | Shooting time |
| DateTime | Modification time |

standardsLinks.title: Standards and specs
standardsLinks.desc: EXIF specification links: CIPA/JEITA Exif standard, LOC JPEG/TIFF family docs, tag lists—official sources for parsers.
standardsLinks.content:
When implementing readers, writers, or checking tag/version behavior, you need the actual specs. Here are links and one-line notes only; no copied text—rely on the official sources.

**CIPA** (with JEITA) maintains the Exif standard.

- [CIPA standards (Exif DC-008)](https://www.cipa.jp/e/std/std-sec.html) — Current Exif spec and downloads.
- [CIPA update history](https://www.cipa.jp/e/std/history_sec.html) — Version history.

**Library of Congress (LOC)**

- [Exchangeable Image File Format (Exif) Family](https://www.loc.gov/preservation/digital/formats/fdd/fdd000618.shtml) — Overview, versions, use in JPEG/TIFF.
- [Tags for TIFF and Related Specifications](https://www.loc.gov/preservation/digital/formats/content/tiff_tags.shtml) — TIFF/Exif tag list (Exif, GPS, Interop).

**Further** Wikipedia [Exif](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exif) is a useful intro; for exact tag and format behavior, use CIPA/JEITA and LOC.

## EXIF specification vs “JPEG with EXIF”

When people search for **EXIF specification** or **JPEG EXIF format**, they usually mean (1) the **CIPA/JEITA Exif standard**—the normative tag dictionary and IFD rules—and (2) the **on-disk layout** where a normal JPEG bitstream includes an **APP1** segment embedding a TIFF-like EXIF tree. For IFD structure, read [How is EXIF structured?](/guides/exif-structure); for which file types carry EXIF, see [Which formats contain EXIF?](/guides/supported-formats). ExifStrip is a practical reader/stripper, **not** a conformance lab.

For spec history and versions, see [History of EXIF](/guides/exif-history); for tag meanings, see [EXIF tag quick reference](/guides/tag-reference).

exifSpecificationOverview.title: EXIF specification: official sources and how to read them
exifSpecificationOverview.desc: CIPA/JEITA Exif (DC-008), LOC format notes, and how the normative spec relates to JPEG APP1—without mixing “standard” with “file format.”
exifSpecificationOverview.content:
When people say **EXIF specification**, they usually mean the **normative document** published by **CIPA** and **JEITA** that defines tag names, value types, IFD layout, and GPS/date rules—not a single JPEG sample file. This article explains **where the real spec lives**, how it relates to **JPEG files that embed EXIF**, and what to use when you need **exact** tag behavior.

## Who publishes the EXIF specification?

- **CIPA** (Camera & Imaging Products Association) and **JEITA** jointly maintain the **Exchangeable image file format for digital still cameras** (often cited as **Exif** or **DC-008**). Current downloads and the **update history** are on the [CIPA standards page](https://www.cipa.jp/e/std/std-sec.html) and [history](https://www.cipa.jp/e/std/history_sec.html).
- The **Library of Congress** publishes **format description documents** (e.g. the [Exif family overview](https://www.loc.gov/preservation/digital/formats/fdd/fdd000618.shtml)) that summarize versions and usage in JPEG/TIFF. These are excellent for **big-picture** understanding; for **implementing a parser** or settling a corner case, **CIPA/JEITA** remains the primary source.

Tools such as [ExifTool Tag Names](https://exiftool.org/TagNames/index.html) are widely used **indexes** of tags—they do not replace the standard text when you need proof of how a value is encoded.

## “Exif standard” vs “JPEG with EXIF inside”

Two layers are easy to conflate:

1. **The Exif standard** — Describes **IFD0**, the **Exif sub-IFD** (pointer `0x8769`), the **GPS IFD** (`0x8825`), **Interop** (`0xA005`), tag IDs, TIFF types (ASCII, SHORT, RATIONAL, SRATIONAL, …), and fields such as **ExifVersion** and **DateTimeOriginal**. This is what **exif specification** and **jpeg exif format** questions often point to: the **rules** for metadata.
2. **A JPEG file that carries EXIF** — A normal JPEG **bitstream** with an **APP1** segment (`0xFFE1`) containing a **TIFF-like** EXIF tree. The [LOC note on JPEG with Exif metadata](https://www.loc.gov/preservation/digital/formats/fdd/fdd000147.shtml) describes how metadata sits **beside** the compressed image data, not inside the entropy-coded scan in typical baseline JPEGs.

You need the **standard** to know what **GPSAltitudeRef** means; you need the **file layout** to know where APP1 begins. For IFD structure on this site, see [How is EXIF structured?](/guides/exif-structure). For link-only official URLs, see [Standards and specs](/guides/standards-links).

## IPTC, XMP, and boundaries

The **Exif specification** does **not** subsume **IPTC** or **XMP**. A single JPEG can hold Exif IFDs **and** other metadata blocks. Stripping “EXIF” in a narrow sense may still leave captions or rights data elsewhere—see [IPTC, XMP, and EXIF](/guides/iptc-xmp-overview).

## What ExifStrip is (and is not)

**ExifStrip** is a **practical reader and stripper** for common workflows: it parses mainstream tags and groups them by topic. It is **not** a conformance test suite for every Exif 3.x edge case or every vendor **MakerNote**. For those, use the official spec revisions and manufacturer documentation.

## Related reading

- [History of EXIF](/guides/exif-history)
- [EXIF tag quick reference](/guides/tag-reference)
- [What is EXIF?](/guides/what-is-exif)

exifDatetimeoriginalVsDatetime.title: DateTimeOriginal vs DateTime vs DateTimeDigitized
exifDatetimeoriginalVsDatetime.desc: EXIF date/time tags: capture time vs file modification vs scan/digitization—IFD placement, format, and common mix-ups in viewers.
exifDatetimeoriginalVsDatetime.content:
Searches like **DateTimeOriginal vs DateTime** or **what is DateTimeOriginal** usually come from comparing three different **meanings** of “time” in one photo file. All three use the common **ASCII** pattern `YYYY:MM:DD HH:MM:SS` (no timezone in the base tags), but they answer different questions. Names and IFDs align with our [EXIF tag quick reference](/guides/tag-reference) and the **dateTime** dimension on this site.

## Quick answer

| Tag | Typical meaning | Where it usually lives |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **DateTimeOriginal** | **When the photo was taken** (shutter open / original capture instant, as recorded by the camera or phone) | Commonly in the **Exif sub-IFD** |
| **DateTime** | **When the image file was last changed** (e.g. save or metadata touch in many workflows) | Often in **IFD0** (“0th” IFD) as image-wide file stamp |
| **DateTimeDigitized** | **When the image was digitized**—for a digital camera often close to capture; for scans, when the scan was produced | Exif sub-IFD |

Authoritative definitions are in the **CIPA/JEITA** Exif specification; the table above is the **practical** distinction readers use.

## Why viewers disagree

- Some apps show only one field and label it “Date taken,” which may map to **DateTimeOriginal** or fall back to **DateTime** or filesystem dates—**not** the same thing.
- **DateTime** updates when software **rewrites** the file or touches metadata, even if pixels are unchanged. **DateTimeOriginal** is intended to stay tied to **capture** unless software explicitly changes it (not all software respects that).
- **DateTimeDigitized** matters for **scanned** material: it can differ from **DateTimeOriginal** if the scan happens long after the physical photo was taken.

## Timezone and offsets

The classic three tags **do not embed a timezone**. **OffsetTime** / **OffsetTimeOriginal** (when present) add UTC offset in newer usage; see [Date and time in EXIF](/guides/dimension-datetime) and [History of EXIF](/guides/exif-history) for version context.

## Privacy and stripping

All of these timestamps can reveal **when** something happened—even without GPS. If you share files, decide whether you need capture time preserved; see [Which EXIF fields are sensitive?](/guides/sensitive-fields) and [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip).

## Related reading

- [Date and time (dimension overview)](/guides/dimension-datetime)
- [EXIF tag quick reference](/guides/tag-reference)

exifVersionFlashpixVersionExplained.title: ExifVersion and FlashPixVersion: 0220, 0221, 0231 explained
exifVersionFlashpixVersionExplained.desc: Four-byte Exif version strings (0220/0221/0231…), FlashPixVersion 0100, who writes them, and what happens after metadata strip.
exifVersionFlashpixVersionExplained.content:
If you see **0220**, **0221**, or **0231** inside **ExifVersion**, you are looking at a **four-byte ASCII field** (often shown as a string) that states **which revision of the Exif standard** the writer aimed to follow—not the camera firmware revision. **FlashPixVersion** is a separate legacy/version field (commonly **0100**) related to **FlashPix** interoperability; both appear in the **image properties** cluster on this site and in [EXIF tag quick reference](/guides/tag-reference).

## ExifVersion (four characters)

- Stored as type **UNDEFINED** (four bytes) in the Exif sub-IFD; readers usually display it like **0220**, **0221**, **0230**, **0231**, **0300**, etc.
- The numeric progression tracks **CIPA/JEITA** specification revisions (2.2, 2.3, 2.31, 3.0, …). The exact mapping of string → brochure version is defined in the **official update history**—use [CIPA update history](https://www.cipa.jp/e/std/history_sec.html) rather than third-party tables when you need precision.
- **Cameras, phones, and software** set this when they write EXIF. Re-saving in an editor may update **ExifVersion** if the software upgrades metadata to a newer profile.

## FlashPixVersion

- Often **0100** for **FlashPix Version 1.0** in files that include FlashPix-related interoperability data. It is **not** a substitute for **ExifVersion**; both can appear in the same file.
- If you only care about photography metadata, **ExifVersion** is usually the one that tracks **Exif** evolution; **FlashPixVersion** matters more for legacy interoperability scenarios.

## After stripping or re-export

If you **strip EXIF** or export “without metadata,” **ExifVersion** and **FlashPixVersion** disappear with the rest of the Exif block (depending on the tool). That does not change pixels in a typical **JPEG lossless** strip; see [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip).

## Related reading

- [History of EXIF](/guides/exif-history)
- [Image properties (dimension)](/guides/dimension-image)
- [EXIF specification overview](/guides/exif-specification-overview)

exifApertureShutterIsoTags.title: FNumber, ExposureTime, ISO, and exposure bias in EXIF
exifApertureShutterIsoTags.desc: Aperture, shutter speed, and ISO as EXIF tags (FNumber, ExposureTime, ISOSpeedRatings, ExposureBiasValue)—types, rationals, and photography terms.
exifApertureShutterIsoTags.content:
This page ties **photography vocabulary**—aperture, shutter speed, ISO, exposure compensation—to the **EXIF tag names** used in files and in our [EXIF tag quick reference](/guides/tag-reference). Tags live mainly in the **Exif sub-IFD**; types follow the **TIFF/Exif** rules (RATIONAL and SRATIONAL are common for fractional values).

## FNumber (aperture)

- **Tag:** `FNumber` — **RATIONAL** (e.g. `28/10` → **f/2.8**).
- **Meaning:** The **f-number** at capture. Smaller numbers mean a wider aperture (more light, shallower depth of field). This is the usual answer for **aperture in EXIF** searches.

## ExposureTime (shutter speed)

- **Tag:** `ExposureTime` — **RATIONAL** seconds (e.g. `1/250` as a rational).
- **Meaning:** **Shutter speed**—how long the sensor was exposed. Longer times blur motion; shorter times freeze it. Related tags (e.g. **Bulb**-style capture) appear in vendor extensions; the standard field for a finite duration is **ExposureTime**.

## ISOSpeedRatings (ISO)

- **Tag:** `ISOSpeedRatings` — typically **SHORT** for the main ISO value in consumer files.
- **Meaning:** **Sensitivity** (ISO speed). Higher values amplify signal (and noise). Some cameras write extended ISO metadata in **MakerNote**; the **standard** tag is still the first place tools look.

## ExposureBiasValue (compensation)

- **Tag:** `ExposureBiasValue` — **SRATIONAL** (signed rational).
- **Meaning:** **Exposure compensation** dialed on the body relative to metered exposure (e.g. +⅓ EV). It complements **MeteringMode**, **ExposureProgram**, and **ExposureMode**.

## Putting it together

These tags describe **how the shot was taken**; they do not include lens **Make/Model** (see device tags) or **Orientation** (see [EXIF Orientation](/guides/orientation-tag-explained)). For a broader overview, read [Capture settings](/guides/dimension-settings).

## Related reading

- [EXIF tag quick reference](/guides/tag-reference)
- [How is EXIF structured?](/guides/exif-structure)

exifGpsReferenceTags.title: EXIF GPS reference tags: latitude, longitude, altitude
exifGpsReferenceTags.desc: GPSLatitudeRef, GPSLongitudeRef, GPSAltitudeRef with GPSLatitude, GPSLongitude, GPSAltitude—N/S, E/W, above/below sea level.
exifGpsReferenceTags.content:
GPS coordinates in EXIF are split into **numeric** values and **reference** (hemisphere / sea-level) bytes. This matches the **GPS IFD** in the Exif standard and the rows for **GPSLatitudeRef**, **GPSLongitudeRef**, **GPSAltitude**, and **GPSAltitudeRef** in our [EXIF tag quick reference](/guides/tag-reference). For a gentler intro, see [Location (GPS)](/guides/dimension-location).

## GPSLatitudeRef and GPSLongitudeRef

- **GPSLatitudeRef** — **ASCII** single character: **`N`** or **`S`**, indicating whether **GPSLatitude** is north or south of the equator.
- **GPSLongitudeRef** — **`E`** or **`W`** for east or west of the prime meridian.
- **GPSLatitude** / **GPSLongitude** — Usually stored as **degrees, minutes, seconds** as **RATIONAL** triplets; viewers convert to **decimal degrees** for maps. You should **not** interpret latitude/longitude without the matching **Ref** tags.

## GPSAltitudeRef and GPSAltitude

- **GPSAltitude** — Height as a **RATIONAL** (often meters).
- **GPSAltitudeRef** — **BYTE**: commonly **0** = **above sea level**, **1** = **below sea level** (per the Exif spec’s sea-level reference). Readers combine **GPSAltitude** + **GPSAltitudeRef** to display signed height relative to the reference surface.

## Privacy

Reference tags do not make GPS **less** sensitive—they complete the coordinate. **Altitude** can still hint at floor or terrain. Treat the whole GPS block as sensitive before sharing; see [Which EXIF fields are sensitive?](/guides/sensitive-fields).

## Related reading

- [Location (GPS)](/guides/dimension-location)
- [EXIF tag quick reference](/guides/tag-reference)

exifFlashTagBitmask.title: EXIF Flash tag: bitmask and decoded meaning
exifFlashTagBitmask.desc: The Flash tag (SHORT) packs multiple facts—fired or not, return light, mode—into one 16-bit value; how readers interpret it.
exifFlashTagBitmask.content:
The **Flash** tag in EXIF is a **SHORT** (16-bit) value that does **not** mean “on/off” alone. The Exif standard defines it as a **bit field**: low bits record whether the flash **fired**, whether a **return** pulse was detected, the **flash mode** (unknown, on, off, auto, …), and additional flags (e.g. red-eye-related bits in defined positions). That is why raw values like **0**, **9**, **16**, **25** appear in exports—they are **packed integers**, not an enum with intuitive decimal labels.

## How software uses it

- **Viewers** (including ExifTool-style decoders) map the integer to phrases such as “Flash did not fire,” “Flash fired, auto mode,” or “strobe return light detected,” using the same bitmask rules as the specification.
- **Photographers** care because it confirms whether on-camera flash contributed to the exposure, separate from **ExposureTime** and **FNumber**.

## Where to read the exact layout

For **bit-by-bit** definitions, use the **CIPA/JEITA** Exif specification PDF. For a convenient **decoded list** aligned with practice, the [ExifTool EXIF Flash documentation](https://exiftool.org/TagNames/EXIF.html#Flash) tracks the same tag.

## Related tags

- **ExposureProgram**, **ExposureMode**, **MeteringMode** — broader capture context; see [Capture settings](/guides/dimension-settings) and [FNumber, ExposureTime, ISO in EXIF](/guides/exif-aperture-shutter-iso-tags).

## Related reading

- [EXIF tag quick reference](/guides/tag-reference)
- [Capture settings](/guides/dimension-settings)
