about.title: About
about.section1Content:
ExifStrip is a tool you can use right in your browser to view, edit, and remove EXIF data from photos. You don’t need to install anything: open the page, upload an image, and you’ll see what metadata is embedded—camera and lens, date and time, aperture, shutter speed, ISO, GPS location, and more, grouped by category.

If the photo has GPS data, the shot location is shown on a map so you can quickly see whether the image exposes where you were. You can edit specific fields or use presets to remove whole groups (e.g. location only, or device-related only), then download the result.

It works on both desktop and mobile, with interfaces in English, Chinese, and Japanese.
about.section2Content:
We built it for a simple reason. EXIF is useful for organizing and remembering where and how a photo was taken, but it can also expose information you’d rather keep private.

Many people don’t realize that the originals they post or send often include precise GPS, device model, and even camera serial numbers; anyone who downloads the file can read that data with the right tool. That can create privacy or safety risks.

There are desktop apps that handle EXIF, but they usually require installation, sometimes cost money, and it’s not always clear whether your images are uploaded elsewhere. We wanted a straightforward, free way for anyone to see what’s in their photos before sharing or uploading, and to decide what to remove, without installing software—and with all processing done locally so your photos never leave your device. So ExifStrip was built around the idea of handling everything locally and never uploading your images.
about.section3Content:
ExifStrip has four main areas.

First, viewing: after you upload, you can browse EXIF by category (device, capture settings, location, date/time, etc.); if GPS is present, the shot location is shown on a map. A "privacy analysis" view helps you quickly see which fields might be sensitive.

Second, editing: fields like author, copyright, and capture time can be changed on the page and saved back into the JPEG, useful when you want to replace identifying info before sharing.

Third, stripping: several presets (remove all, privacy only, GPS only, device only, etc.) let you drop metadata without touching pixels for JPEG, so quality stays the same; other formats such as PNG and WebP can use "remove all" with re-encoding.

Fourth, export: you can export the current image’s EXIF to CSV, JSON, plain text, or hex for backup or for reviewing what to strip. JPEG, PNG, TIFF, HEIC, AVIF, and WebP are supported; capabilities vary a bit by format (e.g. preset-based stripping is focused on JPEG), and the site’s guides and blog explain the details.
about.section4Content:
From reading and editing to stripping and export, everything runs in your browser on your device. We don’t receive or see the images you open; if you don’t send them to us, we don’t have a copy.

We don’t use your photos for training, analytics, or ads. That way you can manage EXIF without handing your photos to anyone else.

The full commitment (including what site-usage data or cookies we use) is in the site’s privacy notice—worth a look if you want to be sure.
about.section5Content:
If you need to view or clean EXIF from photos without installing software, you’re welcome to try ExifStrip. You can start from the homepage; for a fuller picture, check the guides and the blog for tips. For common questions, see the FAQ; for feedback or suggestions, use the contact options on the site.

We’ll keep improving the tool and the documentation—thanks for using it.

faq.title: FAQ
faq.heroSubtitle: Common questions about EXIF, privacy, and how to use the tool.
faq.a1: EXIF (Exchangeable Image File Format) is metadata embedded in photos, which can include device, date/time, aperture, GPS, and more.
faq.q2: Are my images uploaded to a server?
faq.a2: No. All processing happens in your browser on your device. We do not receive or store your images.
faq.a3: We support JPEG, PNG, TIFF, HEIC/HEIF, AVIF, WebP and other common formats. Preset-based stripping is supported for JPEG only.
faq.q4: How do I remove all EXIF?
faq.a4: Choose "Remove all EXIF" and download the cleaned image. Other formats are re-encoded to strip all metadata.
faq.q5: Will removing EXIF affect image quality?
faq.a5: For JPEG with preset-based stripping we only rewrite metadata; pixels are unchanged. PNG, WebP, etc. are re-encoded and may differ very slightly; the difference is usually imperceptible.
faq.q6: Can I remove only GPS or capture time?
faq.a6: Yes. In the metadata block select the "Location" or "Date/Time" dimension and use "Remove selected" to strip only that part of the EXIF while keeping the rest.
faq.q7: Can I process multiple images at once?
faq.a7: Yes. You can upload multiple images, then view and edit EXIF for each in the list, or strip and download the currently selected image.
faq.q8: Is my data sent to your servers?
faq.a8: No. All reading, editing, stripping, and export happen in your browser. We do not and cannot receive, store, or view your images or EXIF.
faq.a9: IFD (Image File Directory) is an EXIF standard structure. Common IFDs include IFD0 (main image), Exif, GPS, and Interop; each holds different tags.
faq.q10: Can I restore EXIF after stripping?
faq.a10: No. Once EXIF is removed and you download the new file, the original metadata cannot be recovered from it. We recommend keeping a backup of the original.
faq.q11: Why do some tags show as "Unknown"?
faq.a11: Some camera makers use custom tags outside the standard EXIF spec. The tool still parses and shows hex and raw values; the name may appear as unknown.
faq.q12: What's the difference between HEIC/HEIF and JPEG?
faq.a12: HEIC is a more efficient format used by Apple and others. We can read EXIF from HEIC; for precise preset-based stripping, converting to JPEG first is recommended.
faq.a13: Yes. Your chosen UI language is stored locally in your browser (e.g. localStorage) and will be used on your next visit.
faq.q14: Can I use this on phone or tablet?
faq.a14: Yes. The site is responsive and works in mobile and tablet browsers. Support for HEIC and large files may vary by device and browser.
faq.a15: For privacy, usage, or technical questions, use the contact email in our privacy section; we will respond as soon as we can.
faq.q16: Does X (Twitter) strip EXIF from uploaded photos?
faq.a16: X’s Help Center distinguishes optional post location from embedded image metadata; uploaded images are processed and GPS-type EXIF is generally not shown to other users—but policies change. For detail with sources, open /guides/faq-social-network and /blog/topic-platform-strip; strip locally first if you need a hard guarantee.
faq.q17: What is the difference between remove, strip, delete, and scrub EXIF?
faq.a17: Mostly wording for the same goal: later viewers should not see the old metadata. “Strip” and “scrub” usually mean clearing metadata blocks; tools differ on IPTC/XMP and on whether PNG/WebP need re-encoding. How ExifStrip handles JPEG presets is explained in /guides/how-to-edit-strip.

blog.heroSubtitle: Tips, use cases, product updates and related topics—to help you get the most out of ExifStrip and understand EXIF and privacy.

topicPhonePrivacy.title: Phone photo privacy: what can you turn off?
topicPhonePrivacy.desc:
Turn off or limit "write location into photos" and photo/camera access in system settings.
Existing photos that already have location still need a tool to strip EXIF.
topicPhonePrivacy.content:
EXIF in photos often includes location, device, and time. Besides cleaning it before sharing, you can reduce what gets written in the first place by changing system settings: turning off or limiting "write location into photos" and controlling which apps can access your camera and photo library. Below we only describe what system settings can do; we don’t go into EXIF format details.

**Location and photos**

Most phones have a master "Location services" or "Location" switch in Settings. With it off, newly taken photos usually stop getting GPS coordinates. Some devices also have a separate location permission for "Camera" or "Photos": when allowed, new shots get location; when denied, new photos typically have no location. Note: turning off location does not remove EXIF from photos already taken; existing files that already contain GPS still need to be cleaned with [ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com) or similar.

**Photo library and camera permissions**

"Photos" or "Camera" permissions control which apps can read, write, or delete your images. On iOS and Android you can set this per app: no access, access to selected photos only, or full access. Restricting third‑party apps to "selected photos" or revoking access reduces the chance they scan your whole library or upload originals with EXIF. Camera permission controls use of the camera; with it off, the app cannot take new photos, so it won’t create new EXIF either.

**Differences by device and OS**

Menus and labels vary by brand and OS version—"Privacy", "Location services", or per‑app settings under "App management". If you can’t find an option, check the manufacturer’s or OS help.

In short: turning off location or limiting photo/camera access reduces new photos carrying location and who can read your library; for existing EXIF you still need to [view](/guides/how-to-view) and then [edit or strip](/guides/how-to-edit-strip) as needed.

topicPlatformStrip.title: How do social platforms handle EXIF in your photos?
topicPlatformStrip.desc:
How X, Meta, WeChat, Weibo, etc. typically handle uploaded photos; with reference links.
"Not shown to others" doesn't mean "not collected"—strip before upload to stay in control.
topicPlatformStrip.content:
After you upload a photo to a social platform, the service usually processes the file: some strip or alter EXIF, others read it first and then decide what to show. Policies differ by platform and can change; below we summarize common, publicly reported behaviour and add reference links. Regardless of platform behaviour, **cleaning EXIF yourself before upload** keeps you in better control of who gets what.

## Does X (Twitter) strip EXIF from uploaded images?

People often ask this as a yes/no question. **In practice**, X’s Help Center states that optional **post location** is separate from location embedded in the image file; uploaded images are **processed**, and **GPS-type EXIF is generally not shown to other users** in the way raw embedded tags might imply. That is **not** a guarantee about server-side handling, and product behaviour can change—**strip locally first** if you must not leak camera, GPS, or time metadata downstream.

**Common behaviour (summary)**

**X (formerly Twitter)**

According to X’s Help Center, location on posts is optional and separate from EXIF embedded in the image; uploaded images are processed, and in many cases EXIF such as GPS is not shown to other users. Current behaviour is determined by the platform.

**Instagram / Facebook (Meta)**

Reports and tests indicate that Meta processes photos before what others see; many EXIF fields (e.g. GPS, camera model, timestamps) do not appear on the version shown to others. Meta’s Terms and Data Policy, however, state that they collect information from content you share, including "the location of a photo or the date a file was created"—so the platform may read metadata before deciding what to display. "Others can’t see it" does not mean "the platform doesn’t collect it".

**WeChat**

Media and official responses have noted that sending "original" images can include EXIF (including location); images shared in Moments or after compression typically do not retain full EXIF. Official advice includes avoiding sending originals and turning off camera location to reduce exposure.

**Weibo, Xiaohongshu, and others**

These platforms do not publish detailed, separate statements on whether or how they strip EXIF; actual behaviour may change with updates. The safer approach is to strip sensitive EXIF before upload.

**Recommendation**

Platform policies change, and "not shown to others" does not mean "not stored or used by the platform". If you prefer not to hand location, device, or time to the platform or others: [view](/guides/how-to-view) and [strip as needed](/guides/how-to-edit-strip) with [ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com) or a local tool first, then upload; for important photos you can [export EXIF as a backup](/blog/tip-export-exif) before stripping. More on this in [Before you share](/guides/before-you-share).

*Note: Summaries reflect public documentation and press as understood in 2026; always confirm the current Help Center or policy pages for each service.*

---

# **References**

- [X: Post location – FAQs](https://help.x.com/en/safety-and-security/post-location-settings) (X Help Center, on posting and location)
- [Meta Privacy Policy](https://www.facebook.com/privacy/policy/) (Meta’s explanation of information collected from photos and other content)
- [WeChat original images and location – official advice](https://www.yicai.com/news/100421452.html) (Yicai, 2018, citing WeChat’s official response)

topicExifVsMetadata.title: Is EXIF the same as "metadata"?
topicExifVsMetadata.desc:
EXIF is only one metadata standard; IPTC, XMP, MakerNote briefly explained.
Who writes what, and why "author" can remain after stripping EXIF—links to the guide.
topicExifVsMetadata.content:
People often use "EXIF" and "metadata" interchangeably, but EXIF is only one metadata standard, not all of it. Understanding that makes it easier to see what other metadata lives in a photo and who writes it—and why some tools only remove EXIF while others touch more.

**What is metadata?**

Metadata is "data about data": it describes who created the file, when, with what device, and can include copyright, title, keywords, and so on. In photos, metadata is not the pixels; it is stored inside the file or alongside it, for software, platforms, or people to read.

**Where EXIF fits**

EXIF (Exchangeable Image File Format) is a **metadata standard** for photos (and some audio), defined by bodies such as JEITA/CIPA. It specifies a set of tags—camera model, aperture, shutter, GPS, capture time, etc.—and how they are stored in JPEG, TIFF, and similar files. So **EXIF is one type of metadata**, mainly written automatically by cameras, phones, and scanners when they capture or create the file. For the concept and structure, see [What is EXIF?](/guides/what-is-exif).

**Other common metadata**

- **IPTC**: Often used in news and publishing; focuses on title, author, copyright, keywords, captions. Many stock libraries and editing tools write IPTC. See also [IPTC, XMP, and EXIF: how they fit together](/guides/iptc-xmp-overview).
- **XMP** (Adobe-led): Stored as XML, can be embedded in JPEG, PNG, RAW, etc.; often appears together with EXIF and IPTC; used by Photoshop, Lightroom, and others for edit history, ratings, tags.
- **Maker notes**: Vendor‑specific blocks (e.g. MakerNote) with serial numbers, lens calibration, etc.; only fully interpretable by the vendor’s software.

A single image can contain EXIF, IPTC, and XMP. Some tools that "remove EXIF" only touch the EXIF block and leave IPTC/XMP; if you still see author or copyright after stripping EXIF, it’s likely in another metadata block.

**Who writes what**

Cameras and phones mainly write EXIF when capturing; editing and publishing software often modify EXIF and write IPTC/XMP. After upload, platforms may also alter or strip some metadata. If you want to "remove only location" or "keep only copyright", first [view](/guides/how-to-view) what dimensions and blocks are in the file, then [edit or strip](/guides/how-to-edit-strip) as needed.

In short: EXIF is one metadata standard, mainly for capture parameters, device, time, and location; IPTC and XMP are other standards or formats that can sit alongside EXIF. For a list of common EXIF tags, see [EXIF tag reference](/guides/tag-reference).

## XMP vs EXIF (quick)

**EXIF** = binary IFD tags for capture/device/GPS; **XMP** = XML-based metadata (often rights, titles, Lightroom data). If you search **xmp vs exif**, you are usually asking **which block** still has information after a tool “removed EXIF”—see [IPTC, XMP, and EXIF: how they fit together](/guides/iptc-xmp-overview).

tipBeforeTravelShare.title: Before you share travel photos, clean EXIF first
tipBeforeTravelShare.desc:
Travel and trip shots often carry GPS; posting originals can expose place and time.
Use "GPS only" or "Privacy only" to strip, then download and share—three steps.
tipBeforeTravelShare.content:
Photos from trips or business travel often carry location data that your phone recorded automatically. If you post them straight to social media, anyone who gets the file could see where (and when) the shot was taken from the EXIF. If you’d rather not expose your home area or itinerary, cleaning EXIF before you share is a good habit. With ExifStrip you can do it in the browser, no install.

**Step 1: Open ExifStrip and upload the photos you want to share**

On the [ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com) homepage, click the upload area or drag the photos in. You can add one or several at a time.

![Upload photos to share](/images/blogs/tip-before-travel-share/a1b2c3d4-e5f6-4a7b-8c9d-0e1f2a3b4c5d.png)

**Step 2: Choose a strip option**

If you only want to remove location and keep camera model and capture settings, choose "**GPS only**". If you also want to remove device, serial numbers, author and copyright, choose "**Privacy only**". Then run the strip for the current image; the page will give you a new file with those fields removed.

![Choose GPS only or Privacy only](/images/blogs/tip-before-travel-share/b2c3d4e5-f6a7-4b8c-9d0e-1f2a3b4c5d6e.png)

**Step 3: Download the cleaned photo and share that**

Share the downloaded file instead of the original. Keep the originals as backup.

![Download the cleaned photo](/images/blogs/tip-before-travel-share/c3d4e5f6-a7b8-4c9d-0e1f-2a3b4c5d6e7f.png)

For JPEGs, "GPS only" and "Privacy only" only change metadata, not pixels, so quality stays the same. For HEIC or other formats you can convert to JPEG first and then do the same, or use "Remove all" (which strips by re-encoding). More on formats: [ExifStrip supported image formats](/blog/product-supported-formats).

tipBatchClean.title: Batch cleaning EXIF from many photos: how to go faster
tipBatchClean.desc:
Upload many at once, then work through the list: pick preset, strip, download per image.
Same preset for the batch makes the flow quick once you're used to it.
tipBatchClean.content:
When you need to share a dozen or more photos, opening a tool and processing each one separately is slow. ExifStrip lets you upload many at once, then work through the list: pick a preset, strip, download, move to the next. Much faster than doing them one by one.

**Upload all the photos you want to process**

On [ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com) you can select multiple images in one go or drag several in together. After upload, a file list appears on the left (or top); click a file and the right side shows that image’s EXIF and actions.

![List after uploading multiple photos](/images/blogs/tip-batch-clean/d4e5f6a7-b8c9-4d0e-1f2a-3b4c5d6e7f8a.png)

**Pick a preset, then strip and download each**

In the strip area, choose a preset (e.g. "GPS only" or "Privacy only"), run strip on the current image, and download. Then select the next in the list, same preset, strip, download, and repeat. Once you’re used to it, the same preset for a batch goes quickly.

![Select preset and download for current image](/images/blogs/tip-batch-clean/e5f6a7b8-c9d0-4e1f-2a3b-4c5d6e7f8a9b.png)

When you're done, keep the cleaned downloads separate from originals so you don't share the wrong file. 

There’s usually a "Add more" or similar control near the list; use it to add another batch without refreshing. When you’re done, keep the cleaned downloads separate from originals so you don’t share the wrong file.

Worth trying the preset on one or two images first, then running through the rest. You can also use the privacy analysis or scan by dimension to see what’s in each photo before deciding which preset to use.

tipKeepWhatYouNeed.title: Only want to remove GPS and keep the rest?
tipKeepWhatYouNeed.desc:
To hide "where" but keep device and capture settings, use "GPS only".
For JPEG we only change metadata; quality stays the same.
tipKeepWhatYouNeed.content:
Sometimes you don’t want people to see where a photo was taken, but you do want to keep the camera or phone model, aperture, shutter speed, and so on—for your own reference or for others. In that case you only need to remove the location (GPS) from EXIF and leave the rest. ExifStrip’s "**GPS only**" preset does exactly that.

**After uploading, choose "GPS only" in the strip section**

Open [ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com) and upload the image. In the strip area, select the preset "**GPS only**" and run strip for the current image.

![Select GPS only preset](/images/blogs/tip-keep-what-you-need/f6a7b8c9-d0e1-4f2a-3b4c-5d6e7f8a9b0c.png)

The new file will have latitude, longitude, altitude and other location fields removed; device, capture settings, date/time, author and copyright stay. For JPEG we only change the metadata segment, so quality is unchanged. Download that file and share it.

If you also want to remove device model, serial numbers, author info and the like, use "Privacy only" instead; to clear everything, use "Remove all". For the difference between presets and when to use which, see [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip) and [What to do before sharing?](/guides/before-you-share).

tipExportExif.title: View first, then decide: export EXIF for backup or audit
tipExportExif.desc:
Export EXIF (CSV/JSON/TXT/HEX) before stripping so you can check "what was there" later.
Or export first, review, then choose which preset or fields to remove.
tipExportExif.content:
Before you strip or delete EXIF, exporting a copy gives you a record of what the file originally contained—handy later if you need to check. Or export first, review it, then decide which preset or fields to remove. ExifStrip can export the current image’s EXIF as CSV, JSON, TXT, or hex (HEX); the flow is straightforward.

**After opening an image, find "Export" on the page**

Once you’ve uploaded or opened an image on [ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com), the page shows its EXIF by dimension. The export control is usually near the file info or metadata area; click it to choose a format.

![Export format options for EXIF](/images/blogs/tip-export-exif/a7b8c9d0-e1f2-4a3b-4c5d-6e7f8a9b0c1d.png)

**Pick a format and download**

- **CSV**: Opens easily in a spreadsheet; you can merge data from multiple images for comparison.
- **JSON**: Easy for scripts or programs; good for backup or further processing.
- **TXT**: Plain text, small, easy to search and archive.
- **HEX**: Raw hexadecimal; useful for debugging or comparing with the spec.

The export contains the same dimensions and fields shown on the page, no more, no less. Export only creates a file on your machine; nothing is uploaded to us.

A good habit: before sharing or batch stripping, export EXIF for important photos as backup, then strip. That way you have a record of what was there and whether you removed what you intended.

tipRemoveExifHeicIphone.title: Remove EXIF from iPhone HEIC photos before you share
tipRemoveExifHeicIphone.desc: HEIC from iPhone often includes GPS and device info; view it in the browser, then strip—JPEG presets work best after converting to JPEG.
tipRemoveExifHeicIphone.content:
iPhone photos are often **HEIC** (HEIF). They can hold the same kinds of metadata people associate with "EXIF": **location**, **capture time**, **camera model**, and more. Before you post originals to chat, forums, or marketplaces, it helps to **open the file** and see what is inside.

## View HEIC on ExifStrip

Go to [ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com), upload the `.heic` file, and read metadata by dimension—same flow as JPEG. If there is GPS, you will see it on the map.

## Strip metadata: what works today

**JPEG** on ExifStrip supports **preset stripping** (GPS only, privacy, device only, remove all) **without re-encoding** the pixels. **HEIC** uses a different container than JPEG; **preset-based strip** is not the same as on JPEG. For HEIC, our **"remove all"** path may **re-encode** the image to drop metadata—see [Supported image formats](/blog/product-supported-formats) and the [RAW / HEIC FAQ](/guides/faq-raw-heic).

**Practical workflow:** If you need the same **fine-grained presets** as JPEG, **export the photo as JPEG** first (Photos app or any converter), then upload the JPEG to ExifStrip and run **GPS only** or **Privacy** preset. If you accept a re-encode, try **remove all** on HEIC directly.

## Related

- [Tip: only remove GPS](/blog/tip-keep-what-you-need)
- [Which formats contain EXIF?](/guides/supported-formats)

tipRemoveExifPng.title: Remove EXIF from PNG: what to expect
tipRemoveExifPng.desc: PNG may embed EXIF or XMP in chunks; on ExifStrip, full removal usually means re-encoding—unlike JPEG preset strip.
tipRemoveExifPng.content:
PNG files do not use the same single **APP1 EXIF block** as JPEG. Metadata may live in **several chunks** (e.g. EXIF, XMP, text chunks). That is why **"remove everything except GPS"** is harder than on JPEG—you must edit specific chunks, not one contiguous segment.

## What ExifStrip can do

- **View**: If the PNG contains parseable EXIF-related data, we show it by dimension like other formats.
- **Remove all metadata**: Choosing **remove all** typically **re-encodes** the PNG so chunks carrying metadata are omitted. There is usually a small quality/size tradeoff from an extra encode step.
- **Preset strip (GPS only, privacy, etc.)**: These presets are **designed around JPEG's metadata layout**. For PNG, use **remove all** if you need a clean file, or convert to JPEG first if you want preset behavior—see [Supported image formats](/blog/product-supported-formats).

## Habit

Before sharing a PNG from design tools or screenshots, **upload once** to see if anything sensitive is attached, then decide whether to strip.

## Related

- [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip)
- [Is EXIF the same as metadata?](/blog/topic-exif-vs-metadata)

topicExiftoolVsOnline.title: ExifTool vs online EXIF tools: when each makes sense
topicExiftoolVsOnline.desc: ExifTool online? ExifStrip is not ExifTool—CLI batch vs browser JPEG presets. Compare exiftool.org with no-install viewers.
topicExiftoolVsOnline.content:
[ExifTool](https://exiftool.org/) by Phil Harvey is the **reference** many power users rely on: huge tag coverage, **batch** processing, directory trees, copying metadata between files, geotagging workflows, and output formats like JSON and CSV from the command line. It is **not** a replacement for a website—and a website is **not** a replacement for ExifTool. They solve overlapping but different jobs.

## Is ExifStrip the same as “online ExifTool”?

**No.** ExifTool is a **specific** open-source project and trademarked ecosystem around Phil Harvey’s CLI. **ExifStrip** is an independent browser app with a different parser and feature set—we are **not** affiliated with or endorsed by the ExifTool project. If you searched **exiftool online** or **online exiftool**, you probably want **install-free metadata work**: ExifStrip focuses on **local-in-browser** preview (including maps), **JPEG preset stripping** without re-encoding pixels, and quick exports; ExifTool focuses on **scriptable depth** and **every tag** once installed. For advanced command-line options (for example `-all=` vs `-all:all=`), see [ExifTool `-all=` vs `-all:all=` explained](/blog/topic-exiftool-all-vs-allall) and ExifTool’s official [application documentation](https://exiftool.org/exiftool_pod.html)—behavior can change between releases.

## When ExifTool shines

- **Thousands of files** (events, archives) where scripting `-r`, `-ext`, or `-tagsFromFile` saves hours.
- **Odd formats** or **maker notes** where you need the latest tag database.
- **Automation** on your own machine (backups, DAM pipelines, CI).

Install once, read the [application documentation](https://exiftool.org/exiftool_pod.html), and you get depth no browser app can match.

## When an online tool shines

- **No install**: school or work machine, or you only fix a few images.
- **Quick visual check**: map for GPS, grouped dimensions, export to CSV/JSON for a single file.
- **JPEG preset strip in the browser** without touching the shell—[ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com) keeps files **on your device** (no upload to us).

## Privacy for both

- **ExifTool**: runs **locally**; you control where files go.
- **ExifStrip**: processing runs **in your browser**; pick a tool you trust and read its privacy policy.

## Takeaway

Use **ExifTool** when you live in the terminal and need scale. Use **ExifStrip** when you want a fast, visual pass on a handful of photos—especially **JPEG**—before sharing. Many people use **both** at different times.

## On this site

- [What can ExifStrip do?](/guides/what-exifstrip-does)
- [IPTC, XMP, and EXIF](/guides/iptc-xmp-overview)
- [ExifTool `-all=` vs `-all:all=`](/blog/topic-exiftool-all-vs-allall)
- [Strip metadata from JPEG](/blog/tip-strip-metadata-from-jpeg)

productWelcome.title: Welcome to ExifStrip
productWelcome.desc:
View, edit, strip, and export EXIF; everything runs locally, images never uploaded.
A good first read; links to guides and FAQ at the end.
productWelcome.content:
ExifStrip is a tool you use right in your browser to view, edit, and remove EXIF data from your photos. No install—just open the site and go. Everything runs on your own device, so your images never get sent to our servers. Here’s a quick look at what it can do, what happens to your data, and where to dig deeper.

# **What it does**

**View**: Upload or drag in an image and the page shows its EXIF in blocks—device, capture settings, location, date and time, image properties, author and copyright, and so on. If the photo has GPS data, the capture location appears on a map so you can check it at a glance.

![EXIF by dimension](/images/blogs/product-welcome/c1a2b3c4-d5e6-4f7a-8b9c-0d1e2f3a4b5c.png)

![File info and GPS map](/images/blogs/product-welcome/d2b3c4d5-e6f7-4a8b-9c0d-1e2f3a4b5c6d.png)

**Edit**: Fields like artist, copyright, and capture time can be edited in the page and saved back to the JPEG. Handy when you want to replace sensitive details with something neutral, or overwrite the real info before sharing.

![Editing EXIF fields](/images/blogs/product-welcome/e3c4d5e6-f7a8-4b9c-0d1e-2f3a4b5c6d7e.png)

**Strip**: We offer four presets—remove all, privacy only, GPS only, device only. For JPEGs, preset stripping only changes the metadata and leaves the pixels alone, so quality stays the same; for other formats, “remove all” strips metadata by re-encoding the image.

![Stripping EXIF by preset](/images/blogs/product-welcome/f4d5e6f7-a8b9-4c0d-1e2f-3a4b5c6d7e8f.png)

**Export**: You can export the current image’s EXIF as CSV, JSON, TXT, or hex, so you can back it up or review it yourself before deciding what to remove.

# **Local processing and privacy**

From parsing and editing to stripping and export, everything runs in your browser on your device. We never see or store the images you open; if you don’t send them to us, we don’t have them. So you get the tools without handing your photos to anyone. The full picture is in our [Privacy](/privacy) page.

# **Free to use**

ExifStrip is free and has no ads. The interface is available in Chinese, English, and Japanese. To learn more about EXIF or follow step-by-step instructions, check out [What can ExifStrip do?](/guides/what-exifstrip-does), [How to view EXIF?](/guides/how-to-view), [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip), or the [FAQ](/faq).

productSupportedFormats.title: ExifStrip supported image formats
productSupportedFormats.desc:
What JPEG, PNG, TIFF, HEIC, AVIF, WebP can do on this site: view / edit / strip.
Explains why each format can or can't—structure differs, support level differs.
productSupportedFormats.content:
This site supports viewing and processing EXIF for common image formats: **JPEG, PNG, TIFF, HEIC, AVIF, and WebP**. How each format stores EXIF inside the file differs, so the level of support for "view", "edit", and "strip by preset" differs too: some formats let us change only the metadata and leave pixels untouched; others require re-encoding the whole image to drop metadata. Below we explain what is supported and **why**. For format and standard-level background, see [Which formats contain EXIF?](/guides/supported-formats).

# **JPEG**

JPEG has the fullest support on this site.

**View**: Supported. In JPEG, EXIF lives in a fixed **APP1 application segment** (marker 0xFFE1) as one contiguous block of TIFF-style data. The site parses it and shows it by dimension—device, capture settings, location, date/time, image properties, author and copyright, and so on. If GPS is present, the capture location is shown on a map.

**Edit**: Supported. Fields such as Artist, Copyright, and capture time can be edited in the UI and **written back** to the JPEG. The reason is the same: that data lives entirely in the APP1 segment; we rewrite only that segment and leave the compressed image bytes unchanged, so quality is unchanged.

**Strip**: All four presets—**remove all**, **privacy only**, **GPS only**, **device only**—are supported. We only rewrite or remove the corresponding tags inside the APP1 segment and do not re-encode the image, so quality is unchanged. That is why JPEG is the only format that supports "strip by preset" precisely: its metadata and image data are in separate segments, and we only touch the metadata segment.

# **PNG / WebP**

**View**: Well supported. If the file contains EXIF or similar metadata (e.g. PNG tEXt/zTXt, WebP EXIF chunk), the site parses and displays it by dimension—device, capture settings, location, date/time, etc.—the same way as for JPEG.

**Edit**: **Not supported** for writing metadata back into PNG or WebP. In these formats, metadata is stored in **multiple separate chunks**; the layout is not a single APP1-like segment as in JPEG. Writing back would require rewriting those chunks according to each format’s spec. The site currently implements write-back only for JPEG, so PNG/WebP are view-only for now.

**Strip**: If you choose "**remove all**", the site **re-encodes the whole image** and omits metadata in the output. That is because metadata in PNG/WebP is spread across multiple chunks; to "remove only some" or "strip by preset" (e.g. only GPS or only device) we would have to parse and rewrite or drop specific chunks, which is not implemented. So the only way to remove metadata entirely is to re-encode and leave out those chunks. **Preset-based stripping** and **single-field removal** are therefore not available for these formats. Re-encoding usually has a small impact on quality but adds one more encode step.

# **HEIC / HEIF**

Common on Apple devices; metadata lives in **ISOBMFF container** boxes, not in a single segment like JPEG.

**View**: Supported. The site reads EXIF-related boxes from the container and displays device, capture settings, location, date/time, etc., the same as for JPEG.

**Edit**: **Not supported** for writing back to HEIC. The container’s box structure and write rules differ from JPEG; implementing it would require rewriting or adding boxes per HEIC/ISOBMFF spec, which is not done yet.

**Strip**: **Preset-based stripping** is not supported. To remove only GPS or only device while keeping the rest would require rewriting the right boxes inside the container, which is not implemented. If you want to remove EXIF with minimal impact on quality, convert HEIC to JPEG first and then use the site on the JPEG—then "remove all" or any preset only touches the metadata segment and does not re-encode pixels.

# **TIFF / AVIF**

**View**: Supported; existing EXIF is read and shown by dimension, same as JPEG.

**Edit / Strip**: TIFF also uses IFD-based EXIF in the spec, so in principle metadata could be rewritten without re-encoding; AVIF is box-based like HEIC. On this site, **preset-based stripping** and **write-back** for TIFF and AVIF are limited—either not implemented or "remove all" is done via re-encoding. Exact behaviour depends on the current release. For the full edit-and-preset-strip experience, converting to JPEG and then using the site is recommended.

---

The above reflects the current version and the reasons for each behaviour; it may change with updates. The homepage "Supported formats" section and [Which formats contain EXIF?](/guides/supported-formats) stay in sync with this; if a format does not parse or an option is missing, check file type and size, or see the [FAQ](/faq).

tipStripMetadataFromJpeg.title: Strip metadata from JPEG: EXIF, IPTC, XMP, and what happens to quality
tipStripMetadataFromJpeg.desc: JPEG is more than pixels: EXIF in APP1, plus IPTC and XMP. When stripping is lossless, when it re-encodes, and how presets map to real blocks.
tipStripMetadataFromJpeg.content:
People say “strip EXIF from a JPEG,” but a typical camera or export JPEG often carries **more than one kind of embedded metadata**. If your goal is privacy or a clean file before upload, it helps to know **what can live in the file**, **which tools touch which blocks**, and **whether pixels are re-encoded**. This post stays close to common file structure and ExifTool’s own documentation—**verify details** for your exact software version and workflow.

## What “metadata in a JPEG” usually means

A baseline JPEG file is a sequence of **segments**: markers like `0xFFD8` (start of image), then **application segments** (APPn), **quantization**, **Huffman**, and finally the compressed image data. The EXIF data most photographers talk about is commonly stored in an **APP1** segment (often marker `0xFFE1`) as a **TIFF-style** structure inside the JPEG wrapper—not “mixed into” the compressed pixels themselves. For a structured overview, see [How is EXIF structured?](/guides/exif-structure) and [Which formats contain EXIF?](/guides/supported-formats).

That does **not** mean “everything sensitive is only in EXIF.” The same JPEG can also hold:

- **IPTC** (caption, keywords, credit—common in editorial workflows)
- **XMP** (often XML-based; rights, titles, Lightroom or Camera Raw sidecars embedded in-file)
- **Vendor-specific** blocks (e.g. MakerNote inside EXIF, or extra APP segments)

So searching **strip metadata from JPEG** or **remove metadata from images** is broader than **remove EXIF**. The right mental model is: **several metadata families can coexist**; clearing one family does not automatically clear the others. For a side-by-side view, read [IPTC, XMP, and EXIF: how they fit together](/guides/iptc-xmp-overview).

## EXIF-only strip vs “everything embedded”

Many tools advertise “EXIF remover” or “delete EXIF.” In practice:

- Some utilities only rewrite or drop the **EXIF (APP1 / TIFF IFD)** area.
- Others walk **IPTC** and **XMP** as well, or remove whole **segments**.

If you “removed EXIF” but a stock agency still sees a **copyright** string, that value may live in **IPTC** or **XMP**, not in the EXIF block you cleared. Always **inspect the output file** with the same kind of viewer you trust for sharing—see [How to view EXIF?](/guides/how-to-view).

## Image quality: when stripping is lossless for JPEG

**Key idea:** if a tool only **rewrites or removes metadata segments** and leaves the **entropy-coded image data** untouched, **pixels do not change**—so there is **no new JPEG compression** and no quality loss from stripping.

That is the usual case for **metadata-only** edits on JPEG when implemented as “rewrite APP1 / related segments, keep scan data.” It matches how we describe preset stripping for JPEG on this site in [Does removing EXIF affect image quality?](/guides/faq-remove-safe) and [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip).

By contrast, if a workflow **decodes the image to pixels and encodes a new JPEG** to drop metadata (common on some formats where metadata is scattered across chunks), that **is** a new encode—you can get **generation loss** or different file size even when settings look “the same.”

## A practical workflow before you share

1. **Open the file** in a trustworthy viewer and skim **device, GPS, time, author/copyright**—our UI groups these by “dimension” so you do not miss a block you care about.
2. **Decide scope**: only GPS, broader privacy fields, or **remove all** metadata families we support for that format.
3. **Download the result** and, if the stakes are high, **open the download once more** to confirm nothing sensitive remains.

On [ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com), **JPEG** supports **four strip presets** (remove all, privacy, GPS only, device only) designed around **rewriting metadata without re-encoding** the image for those presets—see [What can ExifStrip do?](/guides/what-exifstrip-does).

## When people reach for ExifTool instead

[ExifTool](https://exiftool.org/) is the usual **command-line** answer for **batch** jobs, **scripted** pipelines, and **tag-accurate** control. It is not “more honest” than a browser tool—it is **different**: you trade installation and syntax for scale. If you are comparing **mass-delete** syntaxes such as `-all=` versus `-all:all=`, read our focused note in [ExifTool `-all=` vs `-all:all=`](/blog/topic-exiftool-all-vs-allall) **and** ExifTool’s [official application documentation](https://exiftool.org/exiftool_pod.html)—flags evolve between releases.

## Backup habit

Stripping is **destructive** with respect to metadata: after you distribute a cleaned file, **you cannot reconstruct** the removed tags from that copy. If you might need provenance later, **export or archive** metadata first—see [Export EXIF for backup](/blog/tip-export-exif)—then strip.

## Takeaway

**“Strip metadata from JPEG”** is a **cluster of operations**, not a single switch: **EXIF**, **IPTC**, **XMP**, and vendor data can sit in different places. For **JPEG**, metadata-only stripping can usually avoid touching pixels; for **other formats** on our site, behavior differs—see [Supported image formats](/blog/product-supported-formats). Start from [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip), then choose the tool that matches **your batch size, privacy model, and format**.

topicExiftoolAllVsAllall.title: ExifTool `-all=` vs `-all:all=`: what the difference actually is
topicExiftoolAllVsAllall.desc: Not two spellings of the same delete: group:tag syntax, block vs per-tag removal, and where the official pod explains it.
topicExiftoolAllVsAllall.content:
Search forums and you will see both **`exiftool -all=`** and **`exiftool -all:all=`** in recipes for clearing metadata. They look alike, but ExifTool parses them through its **`GROUP:TAG`** rules, not as free-form English. This article summarizes what that means for **deleting** metadata and points to the **authoritative** text on [exiftool.org](https://exiftool.org/exiftool_pod.html). **Always confirm behavior for your installed version**—examples evolve across releases.

## The syntax you are really typing: `GROUP:TAG`

ExifTool addresses metadata with **tag names**, optional **group** prefixes, optional **family** numbers, and colons—examples appear throughout the official **application documentation** (same page as above). Among the special names, documentation describes a tag called **`All`** (spelling/casing as in the manual) that can stand for **all tags** in a chosen scope.

That is why “delete meta” examples often show a form like **`-All=`** in the manual’s writing section: it is a **structured** delete, not a vague English sentence.

## What `-all=` usually refers to

In the official **writing examples**, a minimal pattern to remove metadata from a normal image is shown similar to:

`exiftool -all= dst.jpg`

(Real workflows add **output options** such as `-overwrite_original` or write to a new path—here we only talk about the **tag side**.)

In plain language, **`-all=` is the widely copied, documented way to request “delete all meta information from this file”** for formats where that operation is appropriate. The same documentation **warns** against blindly doing this to most **RAW** files (except DNG) because vendor **MakerNotes** may contain data required to interpret the image—read that warning in the official pod before scripting.

## How `-all:all=` differs: two colons, two roles

Here the token is **not** “all equals” twice. It is **`GROUP:TAG=`**:

- The first **`all`** is a **group** name in ExifTool’s group system (the manual lists groups and families; `-listg` / `-listd` exist for discovery).
- The second **`all`** corresponds to the **`All`** tag in that addressing scheme—i.e. **all tags within that group scope**, not a second English word “all” for emphasis.

So **`all:all`** is **structured addressing**, not “extra thorough `-all=`.”

### Block deletion vs tag-by-tag deletion

The official documentation includes a **note on deleting** metadata: depending on family and group, ExifTool may remove information **tag by tag** or remove **entire blocks/segments**. It explicitly contrasts forms such as **`-2all:all=`** with **`-all:all=`** in that context—**one deletes tags individually in that mode; the other deletes entire blocks** (wording per the manual). That matters for **JPEG**, where metadata can live in discrete **APP** segments and vendor blocks.

**Practical takeaway:** if you are choosing between **`-all=`** and **`-all:all=`** for **mass removal**, you are usually choosing **different deletion strategies** at the group/block level—not picking between two synonyms. For a routine “export JPEG for the web, strip everything I can,” many tutorials still start from **`-all=`**; reach for the **`all:all`** form when you **understand** you need the **block-level** behavior described in the manual (for example after reading the note for your format).

## Copy workflows use `-all:all` too

The same `all:all` pattern appears in **copy** examples: documentation explains copying **all writable tags** to parallel locations with **`-tagsFromFile`** plus **`-all:all`**. That is a **different task** from “delete all metadata,” but it is one reason search results mix the two forms.

## JPEG-specific caveat: not everything disappears “by default”

Even when deleting “everything,” JPEG has edge cases. The manual notes that the **JPEG APP14 “Adobe”** segment is **not removed by default** with a plain **`-All=`** style delete because it can affect appearance, while also warning that **color space** information may still be removed—so **colors can shift**. If you need **pixel-identical** appearance after stripping, read the **Adobe / ColorSpace** notes in the official pod rather than assuming a one-liner.

## How this relates to ExifStrip

[ExifStrip](https://exifstrip.com) is **not** ExifTool; we do not parse the same CLI flags in the browser. We focus on **visual review** (including maps), **JPEG presets** aligned with our guides, and **local processing**—see [ExifTool vs online EXIF tools](/blog/topic-exiftool-vs-online). Use **ExifTool** when you need **scripted** control over arbitrary tags; use **ExifStrip** when you want a **no-install** pass on a smaller set of files, especially JPEG, before sharing.

## Further reading

- [ExifTool application documentation (official)](https://exiftool.org/exiftool_pod.html)
- [How EXIF sits in a JPEG](/guides/exif-structure)
- [How to edit or remove EXIF?](/guides/how-to-edit-strip)
